- Concurrency: If multiple review processes run in parallel, is REVIEW_RESULT mutation atomic/idempotent? The plan does not address any of these lifecycle questions.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the plan: (a) Registry ownership — name the single writer for trigger-mode declarations and the process for re-classification. (b) REVIEW_RESULT lifecycle — define CREATE (when/by whom), CONSUME (which calibration job reads it, at what cadence), and RETIRE (retention window or cleanup policy). (c) Concurrency — specify whether REVIEW_RESULT writes are append-only/atomic to handle parallel review processes safely.

findings.location: Plan section: ## Why, ## What; Scope references 'T10 deterministic-triggered on IaC keywords; T11/T12 on data-change / deployed-system signals'
findings.finding: The plan does not clarify whether the overlay trigger router is itself deployed as part of a long-running or production service, or whether it is a library/CLI invoked on-demand. If it is deployed and serves live traffic (e.g., as part of a review pipeline called in CI/CD or an agent loop), then swapping from 'run every overlay' to 'deterministic-or-LLM routing' is a runtime behavior change that would require a staged rollout, rollback path, and deploy ordering plan — none of which are mentioned. If it is purely a local/library component with no deploy surface, T12 is not applicable. The plan's own text does not resolve this ambiguity.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify in the plan whether the router runs in a deployed/long-running service or is a local library. If deployed, add ACs for: (a) a feature flag or canary rollout for the routing change, (b) an explicit rollback procedure (e.g., flag-flip to revert to 'run all overlays'), and (c) deploy ordering if the registry schema change and router logic must be coordinated.

findings.location: AC4: 'Overlay fire/dismissal is recorded in REVIEW_RESULT for offline calibration.'
findings.finding: Three of the four ACs are cleanly observable in-session (unit tests pass, registry field exists, LLM pass exists). AC4 ('recorded in REVIEW_RESULT for offline calibration') is evaluable in-session only if an existing REVIEW_RESULT schema/fixture is present in the repo — if it is, a test or snapshot check suffices; if not, 'recorded' is under-specified (no schema, field names, or test assertion stated). The phrase 'for offline calibration' could also be read as post-sprint validation, which would be MAJOR, but a benign reading is that the recording itself (not the calibration analysis) is in-session work. The plan does not specify what 'recorded' means concretely enough to be sure.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to AC4 a concrete schema element or field name (e.g., 'REVIEW_RESULT includes overlay_id, trigger_path (deterministic|llm), and was_dismissed boolean fields') and a unit-test or fixture assertion that verifies the field is populated.

findings.location: Scope → 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data.' and 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data.' + Deterministic triggers section: 'T5d accessibility <- UI-keyword count'
findings.finding: Two placeholder/deferred-decision signals exist: (1) The scope note 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data' defers a real design decision — the agent must 'figure out' whether T3/T4/T5d should be deterministic or LLM-routed, since no trigger assignment is given for T3/T4, yet T5d IS listed as deterministic. This creates an inconsistency: T5d is listed both as 'lacking coverage data' and as having a validated deterministic trigger. (2) The plan says LLM-router is used for 'confirming ambiguous deterministic hits' but gives no threshold, signal, or rule for what constitutes an 'ambiguous hit' — an executing agent cannot implement this without guessing. Additionally, the deterministic trigger for T5d is described only as 'UI-keyword count' with no stated keyword list or count threshold.
findings.suggested_edit: For T3/T4, explicitly assign them to the LLM-router path (or state they are deferred/out-of-scope for this ticket). For T5d, specify the keyword list and minimum count. For 'ambiguous deterministic hits', define the condition (e.g., 'a deterministic trigger fires on exactly 1 of N keywords' or 'confidence score below threshold X').

findings.location: Why section: 'Running every overlay on every plan is wasteful and noisy; the router decides relevance.'
Title vs. What/Scope sections
Scope: 12 overlays (T1–T12), 4 ACs covering deterministic triggers, LLM router, registry updates, and observability recording
findings.finding: This ticket touches at minimum: a deterministic trigger module (new interface), an LLM-router module (new interface), the overlay registry (schema change for each of 12 overlays), a REVIEW_RESULT recording layer, and unit-test fixtures — easily >3 files, ≥3 layers, and 2 new interfaces. The plan has 4 ACs but each AC bundles substantial independent work (e.g., AC1 alone covers writing and unit-testing deterministic detectors for T1, T5a, T5d, T10, T11, T12). There is no thin vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP suggested (e.g., 'ship deterministic trigger for T1 only, validate precision, then extend'). The plan is structured as a horizontal big-bang across all 12 overlays simultaneously, which means any single failure (e.g., T5d keyword ambiguity) blocks the whole ticket.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into at minimum: (a) a child ticket implementing the deterministic-trigger path for one validated overlay (e.g., T1 with its 6 bright-lines) as an MVP, (b) a child ticket for the LLM-router path, and (c) a child ticket for registry schema + observability recording. Gate (b) and (c) on (a) passing precision checks.

findings.location: Scope bullet: 'T10 deterministic-triggered on IaC keywords; T11/T12 on data-change / deployed-system signals' vs. Deterministic triggers section: lists only T1, T5a, T5d as validated
findings.finding: T10, T11, and T12 are described in the Scope section as deterministic-triggered, but the 'Deterministic triggers (low-FP, VALIDATED round 4)' section lists only T1, T5a, and T5d as validated. T10/T11/T12 are therefore uncovered by any AC that requires unit-tested precision on fixtures — there is no criterion that differentiates 'validated' from 'unvalidated' deterministic triggers, and no coverage criterion for T10/T11/T12. Additionally, the Scope lists T2/T3/T4/T5b/T5c/T5e/T6/T7/T8/T9 as overlays but neither the deterministic-trigger section nor the LLM-router section explicitly assigns all of them; T2, T3, T4, T5e, and T7 are silently unassigned. AC1 says 'per overlay that has a low-FP signal' which should cover this, but the described deliverables don't clarify which overlays qualify.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit trigger-assignment table in the Scope or a referenced registry mapping every overlay (T1–T12) to either 'deterministic' or 'LLM-router', and annotate which deterministic ones are validated vs. pending validation. Update AC1 to list the exact overlays it covers.

findings.location: Scope section: 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data' vs Deterministic triggers: 'T5d accessibility <- UI-keyword count' (validated round 4)
findings.finding: Minor tension between the Scope section noting 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data' and the Deterministic triggers section listing T5d as a validated, low-FP deterministic trigger ('VALIDATED round 4 — agree 12-13/13'). A reader could interpret these as contradictory: is T5d validated or does it lack coverage data? The two claims could refer to different kinds of data (trigger-precision validation vs downstream-dismissal/coverage data), but this is not explicit in the plan. Similarly, T4 is listed as lacking coverage data in Scope but appears in the LLM-router path under 'T4 compat/destructiveness' — this is consistent, but the 'lacks coverage data' parenthetical is ambiguous about whether T4 is fully in scope for this ticket or deferred.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify the parenthetical: e.g. 'T3/T4/T5d still lack downstream dismissal-rate/coverage data (trigger-precision is validated for T5d; coverage instrumentation is a follow-on).' This disambiguates trigger-validation from calibration coverage.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'A deterministic trigger per overlay that has a low-FP signal, unit-tested for precision on fixtures.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces new testable logic (deterministic detectors + LLM relevance router) and AC1 correctly calls for unit tests on fixtures for precision. However, the testing plan covers only the HAPPY PATH (trigger fires correctly on a positive fixture). There is no mention of: (a) false-positive fixtures — inputs that should NOT fire the deterministic trigger but are superficially similar (e.g. a plan mentioning 'performance' in passing without being a performance overlay candidate); (b) failure/timeout/fallback paths for the LLM relevance pass (e.g. LLM unavailable, returns malformed response, or returns ambiguous verdict); (c) the 'ambiguous deterministic hit -> LLM confirmation' branch — there is no test spec for this second-path logic. The LLM-router path in particular is a new user-facing (agent-facing) flow with no failure/timeout/invalid-input test coverage called out.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to AC1: 'including negative fixtures that should NOT trigger (precision) and boundary fixtures (superficially similar but irrelevant plans).' Add an AC for the LLM-router path: 'LLM relevance pass is tested for: (a) LLM timeout/unavailable — falls back gracefully; (b) malformed/ambiguous LLM response — handled without hard crash; (c) ambiguous deterministic hit correctly escalates to LLM pass.'

findings.location: What section: 'A two-path overlay trigger: a deterministic detector where a low-FP signal exists, with an LLM relevance pass as the fallback'; Acceptance Criteria AC3: 'Each overlay descriptor declares whether it triggers deterministically or by LLM, in the registry.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (two-path trigger router) and a new cross-component contract (the registry now carries trigger-mode declarations). This is a novel architectural decision affecting every overlay (12+ overlays, two subsystems). AC3 partially addresses documentation at the registry level, but there is no mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc capturing: (a) why the deterministic/LLM split was chosen over a pure LLM router; (b) the validation methodology (round-4 fixture agreement) as a durable record; (c) the interface contract for future overlay authors (how to declare a new deterministic trigger, what constitutes 'low-FP', how to add a new overlay to the routing table). Without this, future maintainers have no guidance on how to extend or change the routing logic.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC: 'The two-path routing architecture, the validation methodology, and the contract for adding new overlays are documented in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update, including: the deterministic/LLM split rationale, the low-FP validation bar, and the registry schema for trigger-mode.'

findings.location: Deterministic triggers section: 'VALIDATED round 4 — agree 12-13/13 with the LLM router, ~0 false-positive'; Calibration section: 'track each overlay's downstream dismissal rate (over/under-triggering) per the observability substrate.'
Scope: 'T2/T3/T4/T5d were dropped from an earlier experiment subset and restored — see criteria-registry-reconciliation.md'
findings.finding: The plan is primarily additive (new routing logic, new registry fields). The restoration of previously dropped overlays is acknowledged explicitly via the reconciliation reference. No existing consumer interface or data schema is described as being removed or broken. The plan does not introduce irreversible or destructive operations.

findings.location: Plan scope: overlay trigger routing logic, registry updates, LLM router
findings.finding: The plan introduces no new user-facing UI. It is a backend/pipeline routing change. T5d accessibility overlay is not applicable.

findings.location: ## What, ## Scope, ## Acceptance Criteria

## Scope — 'T3/T4/T5d still lack coverage data'; 'criteria-registry-reconciliation.md'; Deterministic triggers section; Acceptance Criteria AC3 'Each overlay descriptor declares whether it triggers deterministically or by LLM, in the registry.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a meaningful new architectural contract — a two-path routing scheme (deterministic vs. LLM-router) — and mandates that every overlay descriptor in the registry declare its trigger type (AC3). It also restores overlays (T2/T3/T4/T5d) that were previously dropped and references an external doc ('criteria-registry-reconciliation.md'). No ADR or documentation artifact is planned to capture: (1) the rationale for which overlays got deterministic vs. LLM triggers and how the validation round-4 threshold was chosen, (2) the updated registry schema (adding the new trigger-type field), or (3) the calibration/dismissal-rate mechanism added to REVIEW_RESULT (AC4). Because the trigger-type field becomes a required contract for future overlay authors, the absence of a doc/ADR for this pattern means new contributors will have no authoritative reference for how to classify and register an overlay.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an AC such as: '[ ] An ADR (or updated overlay-authoring guide) documents: (a) the deterministic-vs-LLM classification rule and validation threshold, (b) the registry schema change (trigger_type field), and (c) the REVIEW_RESULT calibration fields added.' Also confirm whether criteria-registry-reconciliation.md needs updating to reflect the restored overlays and the new trigger-type field.

findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — AC3 'Each overlay descriptor declares whether it triggers deterministically or by LLM, in the registry'; AC4 'Overlay fire/dismissal is recorded in REVIEW_RESULT for offline calibration'
findings.finding: The plan introduces two pieces of shared/global state: (1) the overlay registry (mutated by adding a trigger-type field to every overlay descriptor), and (2) REVIEW_RESULT records (written per-review for calibration). Neither piece of state has its lifecycle specified in the plan. Specifically: (a) CREATE — who initialises the trigger-type field and when (migration of existing entries vs. new entries only)? (b) UPDATE — is the registry a single shared file/DB table with concurrent write risk, and who owns updates? (c) CONSUME — which components read REVIEW_RESULT for calibration; are they tolerant of missing/partial entries from reviews that predate this change? (d) RETIRE — do REVIEW_RESULT records accumulate indefinitely, or is there a retention/archival policy? (e) CONCURRENCY — if multiple review runs write to REVIEW_RESULT simultaneously (e.g. parallel CI jobs), are writes atomic/idempotent? None of these questions are answered or even acknowledged in the plan.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'State lifecycle' sub-section (or extend the Acceptance Criteria) that specifies: (1) how existing registry entries are migrated to include trigger_type (one-time script vs. on-read default), (2) the ownership model for registry writes, (3) the schema and append/idempotency strategy for REVIEW_RESULT calibration records, (4) consumer tolerance for absent records (e.g. overlays reviewed before this change), and (5) a retention policy or note that accumulation is intentional and bounded.

findings.location: ## Why 'Running every overlay on every plan is wasteful and noisy'; ## Scope 'T10 deterministic-triggered on IaC keywords; T11/T12 on data-change / deployed-system signals'
findings.finding: The plan changes the runtime behavior of an overlay-triggering system (the router that decides which overlays fire on live plans). Whether this constitutes a 'deployed or long-running system' in the T12 sense depends on how the overlay router is deployed — if it runs as part of a continuously-running review service or pipeline that processes real plans in production, T12 fully applies and the plan has no staged-rollout flag, no rollback path, and no deploy-ordering notes. If it is a library/CLI invoked on-demand with no persistent deploy surface, T12 is not applicable. The plan does not clarify the deployment model, so the applicability of T12 cannot be determined from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: If the overlay router runs as a persistent service or in a shared pipeline: add AC items for (a) a feature flag or canary rollout for the new routing logic, (b) an explicit rollback procedure (e.g. revert the trigger_type field or toggle a flag), and (c) a note on whether old and new routing logic can coexist during rollout. If it is purely an on-demand CLI/library, explicitly state this to confirm T12 non-applicability.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7: 'Exhaustive live-dogfood validation passed; soak-evidence recorded; adult-oxide-slave closed'
findings.finding:
Three binary checks fail:

(right_sized / observable_outcome): The plan as a whole is a 10-cluster epic (E0–E9) spanning multiple sessions, not a single-session deliverable. The ACs collectively cover: argparse skeleton, entrypoint cutover, ~20 command ports split across 4 clusters, reconciler rewiring, two file relocations, compat-shim deletion, dispatcher deletion, 107 shell-test translations, and documentation — far exceeding any single working session. AC7 ('soak-evidence recorded') is also outcome-vague: 'exhaustive' and 'soak-evidence' are not defined by a specific duration or metric threshold, making in-session evaluability ambiguous.

(in_session_evaluable): AC7 requires 'soak-evidence' and closing an epic story (adult-oxide-slave), which implies post-session validation rather than a deterministic in-session command.

(right_sized): The plan is explicitly an epic-of-epics ('Execute the approved cluster plan, ONE COMMAND AT A TIME … over multiple sessions'); no single AC maps to a right-sized session deliverable.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) scope each cluster (E0, E1, … E9) as its own ticket with its own, measurable, session-completable ACs, or (b) if this is an umbrella tracking ticket, explicitly label it as such and ensure each referenced sub-ticket carries the measurable per-session ACs. For AC7 specifically, replace 'soak-evidence recorded' with a concrete observable: e.g., 'N consecutive CI runs green on main with no subprocess dispatcher invocations, as verified by grep/test assertion in the suite'.

findings.location: AC2: 'Help/usage/error output byte-identical to the bash dispatcher (goldens pinned before cutover)'; AC3: 'All ~20 live commands ported'; What section: 'port ~20 STILL-LIVE bash commands'
findings.finding:
Three minor ambiguities:

(scope_boundaries): The command list in the Why section includes ~22 named commands (init, fsck, fsck-recover, transition, reopen, claim, compact, compact-all, scratch, clarity-check, check-ac, quality-check, summary, exists, resolve, format, list-descendants, delete, get-file-impact, get-verify-commands, bridge-status, purge-bridge), yet the ticket title and AC3 say '~20'. The tilde and mismatch leave the executor to count and reconcile — a minor scope-boundary gap.

(explicit_ac / no_placeholder): AC2 says goldens are 'pinned before cutover' but does not specify WHERE they are pinned (a file path, a package-data location, a CI artifact) or HOW byte-identity is asserted (a specific test or diff command). E0 references 'byte-identical … via a golden-as-package-data help action' which partially resolves this, but the mechanism is described only in the What section, not codified in the ACs.

(priority_ranked): It is unclear whether bridge-status/purge-bridge (E5) and the reconciler rewiring (E5b/E5c) are essential before E6 deletions or are independent; the cluster ordering implies sequencing but no explicit prerequisite map is given, leaving an executor to infer ordering from cluster labels alone.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Enumerate the exact command count (e.g., '22 commands') in AC3 to remove the ~. 2. Add to AC2: 'Goldens stored in tests/goldens/cli_help/ as package data; asserted via pytest parametrize against subprocess output'. 3. Add a one-line dependency map in the What section, e.g., 'E5b/E5c must precede E6; E6 must precede E7; E8 is independent of E4–E7'.

findings.location: Why section: 'Tier E is the LAST tier of the bash->Python strangler-fig migration'; AC7: 'Exhaustive live-dogfood validation passed; soak-evidence recorded; adult-oxide-slave closed'
## What — clusters E0–E9; ## Acceptance Criteria (7 items spanning ~20 commands, 107 .sh files, reconciler rewiring, docs, dogfood)
findings.finding:
This ticket is epic-scale with all the classic 'too big' signals firing simultaneously:

(a) SIZING: Touches far more than 3 files (107 .sh test files alone, plus cli.py, every ported command module, reconciler, ticket_txn.py, compat shims, engine_env, dispatcher, suite-engine.sh/assert.sh/collector, architecture.md, bash-migration.md). Spans ≥3 architectural layers (CLI/argparse, library/_commands, store/event-engine, reconciler, test infrastructure, docs). Introduces a new interface (argparse cli.py replacing the bash dispatcher). Scope certainty is moderate-to-low ("~20 live commands", "~107 .sh test files" — approximations signal uncertain edges).

(b) PARENT SINGLE-CONCERN: The ticket joins at least four structurally independent goals with an 'and': (1) build a new argparse CLI skeleton, (2) port ~20 live bash commands to Python, (3) rewire the reconciler/ticket_txn off compat shims, (4) translate 107 shell test files to pytest. These are independent goals with different risk profiles, different authors/skills, and different failure modes. It also mixes migration + new interface + test infrastructure + docs in a single ticket.

(c) DECOMPOSITION PRESENT: The plan does define 10 sub-clusters (E0–E9) inline, which is positive. However, these are described as sequencing steps within one ticket, not as independently-trackable child tickets. There is no mechanism (separate AC sets, separate completion gates) that makes each cluster independently closeable or revertible at the ticket-management level.

(d) LEAF COHERENCE: This is explicitly NOT a leaf — it is an epic. No single cluster is broken out as its own ticket.

(e) YAGNI: The argparse cli.py abstraction is clearly justified (replaces an existing bash dispatcher; multiple callers). No YAGNI concern here.

(f) VERTICAL SLICE / SEQUENCING: E0 (skeleton + middleware + subprocess fallback) followed by E1 (entrypoint cutover) before any commands are ported is a reasonable thin-slice approach — this is the plan's strongest sequencing signal. However, the highest-risk cluster (E4: init/fsck/fsck-recover, explicitly called out as 'highest risk') is sequenced FOURTH, after three clusters of lower-risk command ports. The riskiest piece is not de-risked first; a safer sequencing would move E4 earlier (e.g., after E0/E1 skeleton) to fail fast before porting 10+ lower-risk commands. Additionally, E8 (translate 107 .sh tests) is a large horizontal slice batched at the end rather than translated incrementally alongside each command port cluster — this is a classic big-bang risk.

RECOMMENDATION: Promote each cluster (E0–E9) to a child ticket with its own AC set and completion gate. Move E4 (init/fsck/fsck-recover) earlier in the sequence (between E1 and E2) to de-risk it before the bulk of command ports. Translate .sh tests incrementally within each command cluster rather than batching all 107 in E8.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Create child tickets for each cluster (E0–E9), each with its own AC set.
2. Reorder: E0 → E1 → E4 (init/fsck/fsck-recover, highest risk) → E2 → E3 → E5 → E5b → E5c → E6 → E7 → E8 → E9.
3. Merge .sh test translation into each command-port cluster (e.g., E2 includes translating the .sh tests for the commands it ports) rather than deferring all 107 to E8.

findings.location: Multiple locations cited below
findings.finding:
Several coherence issues found across the bidirectional-coverage, terminology, and migration-both-sides checks:

(a) BIDIRECTIONAL COVERAGE — ORPHAN CRITERION: AC item "Help/usage/error output byte-identical to the bash dispatcher (goldens pinned before cutover)" is covered by cluster E0 ("byte-identical to the bash dispatcher via a golden-as-package-data help action"). However, the AC does not cover E5b's deliverable: "rewire rebar_reconciler off ticket_reducer/bare event_append/engine_env" is partially covered by the AC "reconciler + ticket_txn.py rewired off compat shims / bare event_append / engine_env", but E5c's specific sub-deliverable — "delete the bare _engine/event_append.py" — has no dedicated AC line. Deletion of event_append.py is mentioned in the plan body ("delete the bare _engine/event_append.py") but the AC only says 'rewired off ... bare event_append', not 'deleted'. This is a removal-without-observable-proof gap.

(b) BIDIRECTIONAL COVERAGE — UNCOVERED WORK: E9's deliverables include "architecture.md offender table -> empty" and "bash-migration.md §7 DONE" — documentation changes. The AC set has no criterion covering documentation updates. These are plan-body deliverables with no covering AC.

(c) TERMINOLOGY INCONSISTENCY: The plan body refers to the compat shims in multiple ways: "compat shims" (## What, E6), "compat-shim importers" (E6), and "compat shims" (AC). The AC says "compat shims deleted with assert-gone tests" but the body says "delete the shims; assert-gone tests" — this is consistent enough. However, the plan alternates between "rebar_reconciler" (E5b body) and "reconciler" (AC) for the same entity — minor but worth noting for traceability.

(d) TERMINOLOGY INCONSISTENCY — 'dispatcher': The title says "delete the bash dispatcher -> argparse cli.py" and the AC says "dispatcher + machinery deleted" (E7 coverage). The body uses "_engine/rebar" (E7) as the dispatcher's path, but E0 says "transitional subprocess fallback for not-yet-ported commands" without naming what it falls back to. If 'dispatcher' and '_engine/rebar' are the same artifact, this should be made explicit in the AC.

(e) MIGRATION BOTH SIDES — PARTIAL GAP: The AC item "Zero _engine/*.sh; compat shims deleted with assert-gone tests; dispatcher + machinery deleted" covers removal. The replacement side (cli.py as the new entry point) is covered by AC item 1. The .sh→pytest migration is covered by AC item 6. However, for the compat shim migration specifically, the AC verifies deletion ("compat shims deleted") but does not verify that all former shim consumers are successfully rewired to the new path — the assert-gone tests catch deleted files but not broken callers. This is a minor gap.

(f) NO DUPLICATES: No duplicate or near-duplicate ACs found.
findings.suggested_edit:
1. Add an AC covering documentation: e.g., 'architecture.md offender table is empty; bash-migration.md §7 marked DONE' (covering E9 body work).
2. Add explicit removal verification for _engine/event_append.py: e.g., extend AC4 to read 'reconciler + ticket_txn.py rewired off compat shims / bare event_append / engine_env; _engine/event_append.py deleted and assert-gone test green'.
3. Clarify the dispatcher artifact name: add '(i.e., _engine/rebar)' in the AC or body wherever 'dispatcher' is first used.
4. Extend AC5 or add a new AC: 'All former compat-shim importers successfully rewired; no ImportError on full test suite run post-deletion'.

findings.location: Cluster E2 (plan §What) vs AC 'All ~20 live commands ported in-process'
findings.finding: The plan lists 'delete' as a read-ish/quality command in E2, yet 'delete' is a write/lifecycle operation by nature. This is a minor classification ambiguity rather than a hard contradiction, but it could send an implementer in two directions when deciding which cluster's protocol to apply (byte-parity golden read vs write/lifecycle soak). No other genuine cross-section contradictions were found: the sequencing (E0→E1→…→E9) is internally consistent with dependencies (E1 depends on E0's skeleton; E5b/c depend on E5's bridge ports; E6 depends on E5c; E7 depends on E6; E8 on E7); the 'no kill-switch / rollback = git revert' constraint is compatible with the per-cluster commit strategy; the AC 'zero _engine/*.sh' aligns with E7's deletion step; the AC 'zero standalone shell tests' aligns with E8.
findings.suggested_edit: Move 'delete' from E2's list into E3 (write/lifecycle cmds: transition, reopen, claim, compact, compact-all, scratch, delete) to make the classification consistent with its mutation semantics.

findings.location: AC bullets; §What cluster descriptions (E0–E9)
§What (E0 skeleton, E5b/c rewiring, E6 shim deletion, E7 machinery deletion); AC bullets; §Scope
findings.finding: The plan explicitly introduces new cross-component couplings (argparse cli.py over rebar._commands/_reads/_engine_support; reconciler rewired off compat shims; ticket_txn.py relocated) and addresses them: the three-facade / one-impl architecture is named ('three facades, one impl'); the transitional subprocess fallback is scoped to E0 only; assert-gone tests are called out in E6; architecture.md and bash-migration.md updates are planned in E9. No new configurable thresholds or business rules are introduced (this is a migration, not new business logic). The novel architectural decision (argparse CLI replacing the bash dispatcher as the sole entrypoint) is captured in E9's doc update. Coupling, changeability, and documentation sub-checks are all addressed.

findings.location: E0–E9 cluster descriptions; §2 per-command protocol; Acceptance Criteria

Scope section ('rollback is git revert'); E6 ('delete the shims; assert-gone tests'); E7 ('delete the dispatcher'); E8 ('delete suite-engine.sh/assert.sh/collector'); AC: 'Zero _engine/*.sh; compat shims deleted'
findings.finding: The plan performs multiple irreversible/destructive deletions (dispatcher, ~20 bash commands, 107 .sh test files, compat shims, suite engine) and explicitly states 'Tier E has NO kill-switch; rollback is git revert.' While 'git revert' is acknowledged as the rollback mechanism, it is not elaborated as an actual back-out plan: there is no stated procedure for reverting in a production/deployed context (e.g., how consumers of the CLI entrypoint are cut back, how the console_scripts entrypoint is restored, whether the byte-identical help golden can serve as a regression guard during rollback). The plan does use an expand-contract sequencing approach (E0 transitional subprocess fallback → E1 cutover → later cluster deletions), which partially mitigates this — but 'rollback is git revert' with no further detail leaves the reversibility mechanism underspecified for the highest-risk clusters (E4 init/fsck, E7 dispatcher deletion, E8 test suite deletion). Additionally, gratuitous compat is not an issue here — the subprocess fallback in E0 is warranted and time-bounded.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a concrete rollback procedure per high-risk cluster (E1, E4, E7, E8) specifying: (1) which git revert command(s) apply, (2) whether the console_scripts entrypoint can be reverted without a package re-release, (3) how dogfood/soak evidence gates each destructive deletion (link the soak-evidence AC to the deletion commits, not just E9). Consider making the E0 subprocess fallback re-enableable via a config flag through at least E4, rather than relying solely on git revert for rollback.

findings.location: Not applicable — plan introduces a CLI (argparse) and MCP facade, not a graphical or web UI
E0: argparse cli.py skeleton + help/usage/error goldens; AC: 'Help/usage/error output byte-identical to the bash dispatcher'
findings.finding: The plan does introduce a user-facing CLI surface (argparse cli.py replacing the bash dispatcher), so the overlay applies. On (a) criticality: the ~20 live commands are enumerated by name. On (b) non-happy-path: error output is addressed via golden-pinned help/usage/error tests in E0, and the subprocess fallback in E0 handles not-yet-ported commands. On (c) flow entry/exit: entry is via the console_scripts entrypoint (E1 cutover), success exit is in-process command execution, and the fallback/subprocess path covers the transitional abandon case. Minor gap: the plan does not explicitly describe what happens when a user calls the new CLI with an invalid/unknown command after the dispatcher is deleted in E7 — the argparse error path for unrecognised subcommands is implicit in 'byte-identical error goldens' but not stated as a named AC checkpoint. This is minor since the golden-pinning approach in E0 implicitly covers it.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to E0 or the AC: 'Unknown/unrecognised subcommand and missing-required-arg error output is golden-pinned and verified before dispatcher deletion (E7).'

findings.location: E9: 'architecture.md offender table -> empty; bash-migration.md §7 DONE'; Scope section: 'no _engine/*.sh'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly addresses documentation in E9: architecture.md is updated (offender table cleared) and bash-migration.md §7 is marked DONE. The new argparse CLI is the primary new contract/surface, and it replaces rather than adds to the existing pattern — so no new ADR is strictly required for a migration completion. The plan does not mention updating any user-facing CLI reference docs (man pages, README command listings, or --help equivalents in docs/) that currently reference the bash dispatcher by name or path, which could strand stale references. This is minor because E9's exhaustive live-dogfood validation would likely surface any major doc staleness, and the plan's 'byte-identical' golden approach means the observable CLI contract is preserved.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a checkpoint in E9 to grep for any docs/ or README references to the old dispatcher path (e.g., _engine/rebar, engine_env, suite-engine.sh) and update or remove them.

findings.location: E0: 'auto-init/freshness middleware (port _ensure_initialized)'; E5b: 'rewire rebar_reconciler off ticket_reducer/bare event_append/engine_env'; E5c: 'relocate ticket_txn.py; delete the bare _engine/event_append.py'; AC: 'reconciler + ticket_txn.py rewired off compat shims'
findings.finding: The plan mutates several pieces of shared/global state: (1) the auto-init/freshness middleware (_ensure_initialized) — who creates the initialised-state marker, under what concurrency guarantees, and whether concurrent CLI invocations are safe during first-init is not described; (2) the event_append path used by ticket_txn.py and the reconciler is being relocated/deleted — consumers are named (reconciler, ticket_txn.py) but the plan does not state whether the reconciler is tolerant of the interim state where ticket_txn.py has been relocated but the reconciler is not yet rewired (E5b/E5c ordering risk); (3) the compat shims serve as shared-state bridges — their retirement is planned (E6) but the plan does not describe what happens to in-flight operations that may have imported the shims at process start before they are deleted. The plan's 'one command at a time, clean green commit' discipline mitigates some of these, but the concurrency safety of _ensure_initialized and the E5b/E5c consumer-ordering gap are not resolved by the plan text alone and require codebase inspection to confirm no TOCTOU or import-time breakage.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to E0: describe the locking/idempotency mechanism of _ensure_initialized (e.g., atomic file check, flock). Add to E5b/E5c: explicitly state that the reconciler rewire (E5b) must land in the same commit as or before ticket_txn.py relocation (E5c), or that the compat shim for event_append remains live until both consumers are rewired. Add to E6: confirm compat shims are only deleted after all importers in the live process graph have been rewired.

findings.location: ## Scope / ## What (E0–E9 cluster sequence)
AC: 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the only machine-readable error channel'
findings.finding: Three of the four ACs are well-framed and in-session verifiable (exit-code doc + conformance test; event-schema version + forward-compat test; MCP outputSchemas pinned in CI). However, the error_envelope AC is mildly under-specified for measurability: 'covers every command failure mode' requires knowing the complete enumeration of commands and their failure modes, which is defined by the existing codebase, not by the plan. Without that enumeration stated in the plan, an agent can't determine when 'every' has been satisfied — and so the criterion is difficult to evaluate as strictly PASS/FAIL in CI without a companion list or test that enumerates the commands. The other three ACs are all (a) observable, (b) in-session evaluable via CI artifacts, (c) durable end-states, and (d) right-sized.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a companion enumeration of the specific commands whose failure modes must be covered (e.g. 'ticket, sync, log, status, …') either inline in the AC or in a referenced doc, and require a test that iterates over that list to confirm schema coverage — analogous to test_schema_coverage.py.

findings.location: AC (b): 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode'; AC (a): 'author one canonical doc (docs/exit-codes.md or a section in output-schemas.md)'
findings.finding: Two blocking ambiguities exist: (1) The location for the exit-code canonical doc is stated as 'docs/exit-codes.md OR a section in output-schemas.md' — this is an explicit undecided fork left for the executor to resolve. It is not inferrable from convention because both paths are valid and the choice has downstream consequences (links from other docs, schema $ref paths). (2) 'Every command failure mode' for the error_envelope AC is undefined — the plan does not enumerate which commands are in scope, so the executor cannot determine when coverage is complete without investigating the codebase first ('verify whether all commands are covered' is implicit). A secondary minor ambiguity: the plan does not specify what version scheme to use for event-schema versioning (semver, monotonic integer, date-based), though this is more defaultable.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Pick one canonical location for the exit-codes doc and remove the 'or'. (2) Append an explicit list of in-scope commands to the error_envelope AC, or reference the file/module where the complete set is defined. (3) Optionally state the versioning scheme (e.g. 'semver major.minor') for the event schema.

findings.location: Why section: 'a 500-person rollout soon means mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'
Title vs. Plan sections (a)–(d) and Acceptance Criteria
## What (a)–(d) and Acceptance Criteria (4 bullet ACs)
findings.finding: The ticket is structured as a leaf but it is clearly epic-scale. It fails the single-concern test with a structural 'and' joining four independent goals: (a) exit-code contract documentation + conformance test, (b) error-envelope schema extension across every command + documentation, (c) event-schema versioning + forward-compat reducer rule + test, (d) MCP outputSchema parity pin in CI. Each goal touches different files, different layers, and different test surfaces. Sizing signals are all triggered: >3 files (exit-codes.md / output-schemas.md / common.schema.json / event-schema.md / mcp_server.py / test_schema_coverage.py / test_schema_outputs.py at minimum), ≥3 layers (docs, JSON-schema, Python tests, MCP server), new interfaces (versioned event schema, canonical error envelope), and low scope-certainty ("covers every command failure mode" is open-ended). There are >6 implied success criteria when the AC bullets are expanded. No child tickets are defined. Sequencing is a horizontal big-bang: all four tracks are presented as parallel work with no evidence-gated MVP or thin vertical slice to de-risk the riskiest piece (the error-envelope-across-every-command work) first.
findings.suggested_edit: Split into at least three child tickets with explicit sequencing: (1) Exit-code contract doc + conformance test (smallest, done first to establish a pattern); (2) Error-envelope schema extension across all commands + docs (riskiest, gated on (1) confirming the conformance-test harness works); (3) Event-schema versioning + unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule + test; (4) MCP outputSchema CI pin (already partially done per plan — may be a trivial follow-up to (3)). Each child is a coherent single-session leaf. The parent ticket becomes an epic with these as children.

findings.location: ## What (b) and Acceptance Criterion 2; ## What (d) and Acceptance Criterion 4
findings.finding:
Two coherence issues:

1. **Uncovered scope in AC 2**: The body (§What (b)) says "decide now" on error-shape, implying a decision/design step, but AC 2 only verifies an end-state ('error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the only machine-readable error channel'). The design-decision step has no observable proof criterion — it is either silently assumed done or would be an orphan deliverable.

2. **AC 4 / §What (d) weak coverage**: §What (d) says 'close the … equivalence pin fully (test_schema_outputs.py is partial)' — the qualifier 'fully' and the acknowledgment of partiality are significant scope signals. AC 4 reads 'MCP outputSchemas pinned equivalent to the JSON Schemas in CI', which does not specify what 'fully' means or how completeness is verified (e.g., which schemas, which commands). This risks the agent marking the AC green while still leaving gaps.

No duplicate criteria found. Terminology is broadly consistent (the plan uses 'error_envelope' and 'error-envelope' interchangeably — minor but not a blocker).
findings.suggested_edit: For finding 1: Add a sub-criterion or note under AC 2 such as 'error-shape design decision is recorded in the schema/docs commit (not deferred).' For finding 2: Tighten AC 4 to name the exhaustive scope, e.g., 'All MCP tool outputSchemas are pinned 1-to-1 against the corresponding JSON Schemas defined in common.schema.json/output-schemas; test_schema_outputs.py covers 100% of registered tools (no partial coverage).'

findings.location: Plan sections: Why, What, Scope, Acceptance Criteria

AC: 'Canonical exit-code contract documented + conformance-tested'; AC: 'Event schema carries an explicit version + tested unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule'; AC: 'MCP outputSchemas pinned equivalent to the JSON Schemas in CI'
findings.finding: The plan introduces three distinct new testable behaviors — (1) exit-code conformance tests, (2) the unknown-TYPE preserve-and-ignore forward-compat reducer rule, and (3) the MCP pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin — but specifies no failure paths or adversarial inputs for any of them. Specifically: (1) The exit-code conformance test is not described beyond 'conformance-tested'; there is no mention of what happens when a script emits an undeclared exit code (e.g., code 3), or how the test distinguishes a wrong-but-coincidentally-passing exit from a genuine contract assertion. (2) The forward-compat reducer test for unknown event TYPEs only mentions the happy path (unknown type is preserved-and-ignored); there is no mention of tests for malformed events (missing required fields, wrong types, empty payload), mixed streams with both known and unknown TYPEs, or boundary cases like an event whose TYPE is an empty string or a known TYPE with an extra unknown field. (3) The MCP equivalence pin test is described as 'partial' today (test_schema_outputs.py), and the plan says 'close fully', but gives no specification of what equivalence means (structural diff? field-by-field? additionalProperties?) — a tautological snapshot of current output would satisfy the letter of the AC while locking in any existing divergence. This last point is the self-authored-oracle / change-detector risk: if the test is implemented as 'assert current pydantic output == current JSON schema', it will pass even if both are wrong.
findings.suggested_edit: For (1), add an AC row such as: 'Conformance test covers: each declared exit code emitted by at least one command, AND a negative case asserting that any exit code outside {0,1,2,10} triggers a CI failure.' For (2), expand the forward-compat AC to include: malformed event (missing TYPE field), stream mixing known+unknown events, empty-string TYPE. For (3), specify what equivalence means: 'Equivalence test performs a structural diff of each MCP pydantic model's .schema() against the canonical JSON Schema file; test fails if any field, type, or constraint diverges — not a snapshot of current output.'

findings.location: What (a)-(d); Scope; Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding: The plan crosses component boundaries (CLI scripts, event schema, MCP server, JSON Schema system) and introduces a new versioning contract and forward-compat rule. Coupling risk is acknowledged and mitigated: new dependencies flow through the existing SSOT JSON Schema system (an established interface boundary), and the error_envelope extension is scoped to documentation + schema rather than new runtime coupling. The exit-code doc and event-schema version declaration are both lightweight architectural decisions captured in named artifacts (docs/exit-codes.md or output-schemas.md, docs/event-schema.md), satisfying the documentation sub-check. No hardcoded thresholds or rules expected to evolve are introduced. All three sub-checks pass.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria and ## What sections
## What (b): 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode'; ## What (c): 'Declare it formally versioned'; ## Why: 'breaking-change window'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly acknowledges it is changing/freezing contracts (error envelope extension, event-schema versioning, exit-code formalization) that agents already consume — stating 'error-shape changes are breaking — decide now' and 'the breaking-change window … is the cheap time to freeze them.' However, despite acknowledging breakage, the plan provides NO rollback/back-out mechanism and no expand-contract sequencing. There is no description of: (1) how agents on older rebar versions interop during the rollout window while some clones have the new schema and others don't; (2) a rollback path if the frozen error_envelope shape turns out to be wrong post-freeze; (3) any versioned shim or deprecation grace period for the exit-code contract changes. The mention of a '500-person rollout … mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets' makes this a genuine multi-version interop risk, not just a documentation exercise. Acknowledgement of breakage alone is present, but the required reversibility mechanism is entirely absent.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit section (e.g., '## Rollback & Migration') that: (a) defines expand-contract steps — e.g., schemas are additive-only in phase 1 (new fields optional), then enforcement added in phase 2 after all clones have upgraded; (b) specifies a back-out path if the frozen shapes need correction (e.g., bump a schema version, revert the conformance test pin); (c) describes how agents on pre-freeze rebar versions will behave when they encounter the new error_envelope shape from a post-freeze origin (forward-compat fallback or fail-safe). For the event-schema specifically, the unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule (already planned) partially addresses (c) — call that out explicitly as the interop bridge.

findings.location: ## Scope: 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests; no behavioral redesign'
Plan > Scope: 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests; no behavioral redesign.'

AC: 'Canonical exit-code contract documented + conformance-tested'; AC: 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the only machine-readable error channel'; AC: 'Event schema carries an explicit version'; Plan > What (d): 'Close the mcp_server.py pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin fully'
findings.finding:
Three sub-issues:

(a) NEW-needed: The plan creates at least two new canonical documents (docs/exit-codes.md or a section in output-schemas.md; a versioned docs/event-schema.md update) and effectively promotes error_envelope to an architectural contract. The plan names the output artifacts loosely ('docs/exit-codes.md or a section in output-schemas.md') but never commits to a final document structure, owner, or whether an ADR is required for the 'error_envelope is the ONLY machine-readable error channel' decision. That is an architectural decision that warrants an explicit ADR or clearly labelled design-decision section.

(b) INVALIDATED: The plan acknowledges that docs/event-schema.md is the true wire format but does not audit which existing docs or agent README/onboarding pages currently reference exit codes scattered through 'script headers'. Those references will become stale once exit codes are canonicalised. No stale-reference sweep is mentioned.

(c) navigable: The plan mentions adding a version to the event schema but gives no structural guidance (e.g., semver vs integer, where the version field lives in the schema, how the version is surfaced in generated artefacts). A reader implementing the event-schema version field has no template to follow.
findings.suggested_edit:
(a) Add a concrete decision: 'Create docs/adr/NNNN-error-envelope-as-sole-machine-readable-channel.md' to record the architectural decision.
(b) Add a task: 'Audit and update all existing docs/script headers that reference exit codes directly; remove or redirect stale references.'
(c) Specify the versioning scheme in the AC, e.g.: 'Event schema MUST carry a top-level `schemaVersion` integer field (starting at 1); conformance test MUST assert field presence and that unknown TYPEs do not increment the version requirement.'

findings.location: Plan > What (c): 'docs/event-schema.md is the true wire format BETWEEN CLONES of different rebar versions'; Plan > Why: '500-person rollout soon means mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a shared, versioned event-schema state that is consumed by clones running different rebar versions. The forward-compat reducer rule (unknown TYPEs preserved-and-ignored) addresses one consumer-tolerance concern, but the plan does not specify: (a) WHO owns and updates the schema version (which team/role, what process gates a version bump?), (b) whether the version field itself is written atomically or can be partially observed during a rolling upgrade, and (c) there is no RETIRE path — old schema versions are mentioned only implicitly via the forward-compat rule, with no policy for when an old version can be dropped. Whether these gaps are real risks depends on codebase details (e.g., is the schema file written by a single writer or multiple processes?) that cannot be determined from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the AC or a 'Decisions' section: (a) 'Schema version is owned by [team/role]; bumps require [PR approval / release note entry].'; (b) 'Schema file is read-only at runtime; version field is written once at schema-publish time, not at event-emit time.'; (c) 'A version deprecation/retirement policy is documented: versions older than N releases are unsupported and a warning is emitted.'

findings.location: Plan section 'What' items (a)–(d) and 'Why': '500-person rollout soon means mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly acknowledges a mixed-version runtime concern ('mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'), which means producers and consumers of the wire format are deployed independently and will coexist. This makes T12's deploy-ordering and rollback checks load-bearing. However, the plan does NOT specify: (a) STAGED ROLLOUT — there is no mention of a flag, canary, or phased rollout for the error-envelope shape change or the event-schema versioning change, both of which alter the runtime wire format between agents; (b) ROLLBACK — no rollback path is described for any of the four deliverables; (c) DEPLOY ORDERING — the plan says the window is 'cheap' and invokes the announcement date, but gives no concrete ordering rule for when old producers vs. new consumers (or vice versa) can coexist safely while the error_envelope or event-schema version field is being introduced. Items (a) exit-code doc and (d) MCP schema pin are essentially documentation/test-only and carry low deploy risk. Items (b) error_envelope extension and (c) event-schema versioning ARE behavioral wire-format changes affecting agents at runtime, and the mixed-version concern the plan itself raises makes this non-trivial. The forward-compat reducer rule for unknown event TYPEs (item c) partially addresses coexistence, but only in one direction and only for event types — it does not cover error_envelope shape changes or the scenario where a newer agent emits a versioned schema field that an older agent has never seen.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a dedicated 'Rollout & Rollback' section covering: (1) For error_envelope changes — specify that old agents encountering the new fields must degrade gracefully (e.g., additional fields are additive/ignored); define the deploy ordering (update consumers before producers, or vice versa). (2) For event-schema versioning — explicitly state which version field values are valid during the transition and how an old agent that receives a versioned event it predates should behave. (3) A rollback path: since this is docs + schema changes, the rollback is likely 'revert the schema files and re-run CI' — if so, state it explicitly so the executing agent does not over-engineer it. (4) Confirm that error_envelope changes are strictly additive (no field removals or type narrowing) during the rollout window.

findings.location: AC: 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the only machine-readable error channel'
findings.finding: Three of the four ACs are well-formed and in-session evaluable (canonical doc + test, schema version + forward-compat test, MCP pin in CI). The second AC — 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode' — is borderline: 'every command failure mode' is evaluable only if the set of commands is enumerated or discoverable from repo artifacts. Without knowing that list, completeness cannot be verified in-session and the criterion could be declared done prematurely or disputed at review. The other three ACs all satisfy observable-outcome, in-session-evaluable, durable, and right-sized checks cleanly. AC4 ('MCP outputSchemas pinned equivalent to the JSON Schemas in CI') is slightly thin on what 'pinned equivalent' means mechanically (equality check? generated from same source?) but is minor.
findings.suggested_edit: For AC2, add: 'Completeness is verified by an enumeration test (e.g., test_error_envelope_coverage.py) that iterates the full list of CLI commands defined in [entry-point / commands registry] and asserts each has a corresponding error_envelope schema entry.' For AC4, clarify: 'Equivalence is enforced by a CI step that regenerates MCP outputSchemas from the JSON Schemas and asserts no diff (or uses a shared source-of-truth).'

findings.location: AC (b): 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode'; AC (a): 'docs/exit-codes.md or a section in output-schemas.md'
findings.finding:
Two scope decisions are left open for the executor to make rather than decided in the plan:
1. AC (a) gives the executor a choice between two output locations ('docs/exit-codes.md OR a section in output-schemas.md'). This is a real design decision (single canonical location vs. embedded section) that the plan author should resolve; an executor that picks the 'wrong' option may face rework.
2. AC (b): 'every command failure mode' is undefined — the set of commands is not enumerated and there is no pointer to a registry or list. An executor cannot know when they are done without investigating this themselves, which is a 'figure out' placeholder in disguise.
3. Minor: AC (d) says 'close the mcp_server.py pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin fully (test_schema_outputs.py is partial)' — 'fully' and 'partial' are relative, and no specific gaps are named, leaving the executor to discover them.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Pick one canonical location for the exit-code doc (e.g., 'docs/exit-codes.md') and remove the 'or'. 2. Add a sentence enumerating the command set or pointing to a source-of-truth (e.g., 'All CLI entry points registered in rebar/commands/__init__.py'). 3. For AC(d), list the specific pydantic models / schemas that are currently unpinned so the executor has a fixed target.

findings.location: ## Why section: '500-person rollout soon means mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'; '…three contracts agents depend on are unfrozen'
AC (d): 'MCP outputSchemas pinned equivalent to the JSON Schemas in CI'; Plan body (d): 'Close the mcp_server.py pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin fully'
findings.finding: Three of four sub-goals map cleanly to the title ('Contract freeze: exit-code contract, error envelope everywhere, event-schema versioning'). The fourth sub-goal — (d) closing the MCP pydantic/JSON-schema equivalence pin — does not appear in the ticket title at all. It is a related contract-hygiene item, but it is a distinct interface (MCP outputSchemas vs. wire-format event schema / error envelope). This is a mild scope-addition relative to the headline. It does not contradict the intent, but the title promises exactly three freezes and the plan delivers a fourth work item. Additionally, no existing callers of the current (unfrozen) mcp_server.py pydantic models are explicitly acknowledged or listed, leaving it open whether the pin introduces a breaking change for any downstream consumer.
findings.suggested_edit: Either (a) add 'MCP output-schema pin' to the ticket title, e.g. '… + MCP outputSchema pin', so the headline matches all four ACs, or (b) move item (d) to a separate clean-up ticket and keep this ticket strictly to the three freezes named in the title. Also add a brief note on any known callers of mcp_server.py outputSchemas to satisfy the callers-acknowledged check.

findings.location: Plan body items (a)–(d); four independent ACs; 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests' scope statement
findings.finding:
The ticket fails the single-concern / decomposition tests on multiple counts:

1. **Structural 'and' joining independent goals**: Items (a) exit-code contract, (b) error-envelope schema extension, (c) event-schema versioning + forward-compat reducer, and (d) MCP schema pin are four distinct, independently-deliverable concerns spanning different files (docs/exit-codes.md, error_envelope schema, docs/event-schema.md, mcp_server.py + test_schema_outputs.py) and different layers (runtime script headers, JSON Schema definitions, event-processing reducer logic, MCP Pydantic models, CI tests).

2. **Sizing signals**: >3 files, ≥3 layers (schema layer, runtime/scripting layer, MCP server layer, docs layer, CI test layer), and introduces new interfaces (versioned event schema, canonical error envelope contract, exit-code doc). Every 'push-toward-decompose' signal fires.

3. **No vertical-slice / evidence-gated MVP sequencing**: The plan lists work items (a)–(d) flatly with no sequencing rationale. The riskiest piece — (c) the unknown-TYPE forward-compat reducer rule, which touches live inter-clone wire format — is not called out as the highest-risk item to de-risk first, nor is there a thin slice that validates assumptions before the full four-part build.

4. **No child tickets**: For an epic-scale body with four independent deliverables, no children are present.

5. **Leaf coherence**: As a single leaf ticket the scope is likely too large for one coherent session (four schema/doc/test deliverables across multiple layers).
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into at least three child tickets ordered by risk: (1) Event-schema versioning + unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule (riskiest — cross-clone wire format); (2) Error-envelope extension to all command failure modes (second riskiest — breaking shape change); (3) Exit-code canonical doc + conformance test (lowest risk, pure documentation + test); optionally (4) MCP outputSchema pin as a separate clean-up. Gate (2) and (3) on the versioning approach decided in (1).

findings.location: Plan body item (c) 'add + test a reducer rule that unknown event TYPEs are preserved-and-ignored'; AC 3 'Event schema carries an explicit version + tested unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule'; Plan body item (b) vs AC 2; Plan body item (d) vs AC 4
findings.finding:
Several coherence issues:

1. **Orphan work — reducer rule implementation**: Plan body (c) specifies 'add + test a reducer rule that unknown event TYPEs are preserved-and-ignored'. AC 3 covers testing the rule, but no AC explicitly covers the *implementation* of the reducer change itself (only the schema version + the test). The deliverable 'reducer rule code' is uncovered by the ACs.

2. **Orphan work — 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the ONLY machine-readable error channel'**: AC 2 names this, but the plan body for (b) says only 'extend the error_envelope schema to every command's failure mode so agents never parse stderr prose; error-shape changes are breaking — decide now.' The phrase 'decide now' implies a design decision is a deliverable, yet no AC covers the outcome of that decision (e.g. an ADR or documented policy).

3. **Terminology drift**: Plan body uses 'mcp_server.py pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin'; AC 4 says 'MCP outputSchemas pinned equivalent to the JSON Schemas in CI'. 'outputSchemas' (MCP concept) vs 'pydantic-model' (implementation concept) are used interchangeably but are distinct technical things — a pydantic model generates a JSON Schema at runtime, an MCP outputSchema is a declared contract. The criterion's verify step should reference the same entity as the body text.

4. **AC 1 verification gap**: 'Canonical exit-code contract documented + conformance-tested' — the plan body says 'scatter through script headers — author one canonical doc … + a conformance test'. The AC does not specify what the conformance test actually checks (that scripts exit with declared codes? a static lint? a runtime probe?), leaving the verify step underspecified.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add an AC or expand AC 3 to explicitly cover the reducer rule implementation artifact (e.g. 'A reducer/handler function ignoring unknown TYPEs exists in [module] and passes the new test'). 2. Resolve the 'decide now' open question in item (b) by either adding an AC for an ADR/policy document or removing the ambiguity. 3. Align terminology: use 'MCP outputSchema' consistently across body and ACs, and clarify the relationship to the Pydantic model. 4. Sharpen AC 1's verification statement to name what the conformance test asserts.

findings.location: Plan sections: Why, What, Scope, Acceptance Criteria
AC: 'Event schema carries an explicit version + tested unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule'; What (c): 'add + test a reducer rule that unknown event TYPEs are preserved-and-ignored'
findings.finding: The forward-compatibility reducer rule ('unknown event TYPEs are preserved-and-ignored') is new behavioral logic with only a happy-path description. The plan names no failure/invalid paths: what happens when a malformed event (missing required fields, wrong TYPE casing, null payload, oversized event) is encountered alongside an unknown TYPE? A test that only checks that unknown TYPEs pass through is a near-tautology — it validates the happy path of the rule but not the boundary between 'unknown but valid envelope' and 'unknown and malformed'. This risks locking in a silent-drop behavior for malformed events that should instead surface errors, which is exactly the scenario the error_envelope AC is trying to prevent. The plan should explicitly call out at least one negative test: e.g., unknown TYPE with invalid/missing fields, and assert the expected outcome (preserve-and-ignore vs. envelope error).
findings.suggested_edit: Add to AC (c): 'Conformance test includes at least one negative case: unknown TYPE with a malformed/missing required field — expected behavior (preserve-and-ignore vs. error envelope) is explicitly decided and tested.' Similarly, note in What (c) whether the reducer rule applies only to structurally-valid envelopes or also to syntactically broken ones.

findings.location: What (a)–(d); Scope
findings.finding: The plan explicitly scopes the work to contracts, docs, and conformance tests — no new cross-component runtime coupling is introduced. The canonical exit-code doc and error_envelope schema act as interface boundaries (reducing silent coupling), the event-schema versioning decision is captured in docs/event-schema.md (a design-doc update), and the MCP outputSchema pin is documented in CI. The novel architectural decisions (freezing exit-code contract, declaring event-schema versioned, mandating error_envelope as the sole machine-readable error channel) are all captured in named doc artifacts. No hardcoded thresholds are introduced.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (a)-(d) and Scope section

What (b): 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode … error-shape changes are breaking — decide now'; What (c): 'Event schema carries an explicit version'; Scope: 'Anything that changes an emitted error shape lands inside the window'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly acknowledges that error-envelope shape changes and event-schema changes are breaking for agents running mixed rebar versions ('500-person rollout … mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'). However, the plan provides no rollback path, no expand-contract sequencing, and no back-out mechanism. 'Decide now' and 'lands inside the window' are window-of-opportunity justifications, not reversibility mechanisms. If the freeze ships incorrectly shaped schemas or an incompatible version field, there is no described path to revert without again breaking consumers. The missing element is an explicit rollback/back-out strategy (e.g., schema version bump with a supported previous-version alias, a flag to emit old envelope shape, or a documented 'revert commit + re-announce' procedure).
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit rollback/back-out subsection, e.g.: 'If the frozen contract must be revised post-freeze: (1) bump the event schema major version and keep the prior version readable for N releases; (2) provide an error_envelope_v1 alias schema; (3) document the revert commit SHAs agents should pin to.' Alternatively, adopt an expand-contract sequence: first add the new fields alongside the old ones (expand), ship agents that consume both, then remove old fields (contract) in a subsequent PR.

findings.location: Scope: 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests; no behavioral redesign'
## Scope — 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests; no behavioral redesign.'

AC: 'Canonical exit-code contract documented'; 'error_envelope covers every command failure mode; documented as the only machine-readable error channel'; 'Event schema carries an explicit version'; ## What (d) 'Close the mcp_server.py pydantic-model-vs-JSON-schema equivalence pin fully'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly calls for new canonical docs (docs/exit-codes.md or a section in output-schemas.md, docs/event-schema.md versioning) for every new contract introduced, so new_needed is satisfied. However, the plan does not address whether existing documentation that currently describes the unfrozen/scattered exit-code or error-envelope state (e.g., script headers, any existing README or output-schemas.md sections) will be updated or retired. There is a low-but-real risk of stale references remaining after the canonical doc is created without a note to clean up the old scattered mentions. This is minor because the plan acknowledges the source material ('scattered through script headers') but doesn't explicitly commit to removing or redirecting those old references. Navigability is fine given the targeted, small scope of new docs.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit step: 'Remove or redirect all existing scattered exit-code comments in script headers and any prior partial error-envelope descriptions to point to the new canonical sources.' This closes the invalidated-references gap.

findings.location: AC: 'Event schema carries an explicit version + tested unknown-TYPE forward-compat rule'; ## What (b) 'error-shape changes are breaking — decide now'; ## What (c) 'unknown event TYPEs are preserved-and-ignored (forward compat)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces shared/global state in two forms: (1) the event-schema version identifier, which is a shared wire-format artifact consumed by potentially multiple concurrent rebar clone instances (the '500-person rollout … mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets' scenario explicitly named in Why), and (2) the error_envelope schema, which is the sole machine-readable error channel for all agents. For (1), the plan specifies forward-compat read behavior (unknown TYPEs preserved-and-ignored) but does not address: who owns and increments the version number, under what conditions a version bump is triggered, or how two writers on different rebar versions coordinate version stamps on the same event stream. For (2), there is no discussion of what happens when an older agent reads a newer error envelope shape (backward compat for consumers), nor whether the error envelope is written atomically or could be partially observed. These are genuine load-bearing gaps in a multi-version, shared-origin scenario, and they cannot be resolved without knowing how the event stream and error channel are physically stored/written in the existing codebase.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the plan: (a) define ownership of the schema version field — which process writes it and under what trigger (e.g., 'the process that first writes to a ticket's event log stamps its schema version; subsequent writes must not downgrade it'); (b) specify backward-compat policy for error_envelope consumers reading older vs. newer shapes; (c) confirm or add atomicity guarantees for event-log writes in the forward-compat rule implementation.

findings.location: ## What — sections (a), (b), (c), (d); ## Scope — 'Contracts and docs + their conformance tests'; ## Why — 'mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets'
findings.finding:
The plan explicitly acknowledges a mixed-version deployment scenario ('a 500-person rollout soon means mixed rebar versions sharing one origin/tickets') and item (c) directly concerns a cross-clone wire format (event-schema.md is described as 'the true wire format BETWEEN CLONES of different rebar versions'). This makes T12 applicable — the plan touches runtime-observable wire contracts that will be read by already-deployed agents/clones.

(a) STAGED ROLLOUT: The plan declares a 'breaking-change window' but does not specify any flag, canary, or staged rollout mechanism for the new error-envelope shape or event-schema version. All three contract changes appear to flip simultaneously for all traffic once the PR lands.

(b) ROLLBACK: No rollback path is described. Rolling back a schema-version bump or error-envelope change after producers/consumers have already upgraded is non-trivial (it requires coordinated downgrade of all clones). The plan is silent on this.

(c) DEPLOY ORDERING: The plan notes old+new rebar versions will coexist, and item (c) adds a forward-compat rule (unknown event TYPEs preserved-and-ignored), which is a good step. However, the plan does not specify in what ORDER clones/agents should be upgraded (e.g., consumers before producers, or vice versa), nor what happens to an old agent that receives a new-version error envelope during the coexistence window. The deploy ordering between the MCP server (item d) and CLI agents consuming its output is also unaddressed.

The forward-compat rule for unknown event TYPEs (item c) partially mitigates deploy-ordering risk for that specific case, but items (a), (b), and the ordering gap for error-envelope and MCP output-schema changes remain unaddressed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a short 'Rollout & Rollback' section to the plan that covers: (1) whether the error-envelope and event-schema version changes are gated behind a feature flag or rolled out incrementally; (2) an explicit rollback procedure (e.g., 'revert PR; old schema files restored; no data migration required — or not'); (3) a deploy-ordering note specifying that consumers of the new error envelope (agents) should be upgraded AFTER producers (CLI commands), and clarifying what an old agent sees if it receives a new-style envelope during the window. Even a one-paragraph note would satisfy the criterion.

findings.location: AC: 'Full pytest suite green (zero tests/**/*.sh already achieved); opus-review the cutover'
findings.finding: 'opus-review the cutover' is a subjective/effort-framed sub-criterion mixed into an otherwise measurable AC. It is not a specific observable outcome with a deterministic pass/fail signal. All other ACs state observable end-states (files deleted, imports gone, suite green, docs updated, tickets closed) that are in-session evaluable via repo artifacts or CI.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'opus-review the cutover' with a concrete check, e.g. 'no regressions vs pre-deletion test baseline (pytest --tb=short exits 0)' or move the review note to a comment outside the AC list.

findings.location: E7e section: 'callers treat it as one path so __main__.py + console-script path is cleanest'
findings.finding: The plan presents two implementation options for the CLI entry-point fix ('console script via shutil.which' OR '[sys.executable, "-m", rebar]') and then expresses a preference ('__main__.py + console-script path is cleanest') but does not firmly commit to one. An executing agent would have to make a judgment call. This is a defaultable gap (a benign reading sees 'console-script path' as the decision), but it is slightly ambiguous. All other scope boundaries, constraints, ordering (E7e → E7-final → E9), and priorities are clearly stated.
findings.suggested_edit: State explicitly: 'Use [sys.executable, "-m", "rebar"] as the fallback in _default_ticket_cmd; if the rebar console script is on PATH (shutil.which("rebar")), prefer that. Add src/rebar/__main__.py unconditionally.'

findings.location: AC: 'E7e: … live reconciler dry-run vs DIG green' and 'E9 docs updated; adult-oxide-slave + nervy-hold-dip closed'
findings.finding: Most ACs have concrete proving commands (pytest suite, file-existence checks, import deletions). Two ACs rely on external/runtime state: (1) 'live reconciler dry-run vs DIG green' requires a live DIG environment that may not be universally accessible to the executing agent; (2) closing 'adult-oxide-slave + nervy-hold-dip' is a ticket-tracker action, not a repo artifact — its success cannot be verified from CI alone. Additionally, the E9 'Exhaustive dogfood (3 interfaces, fsck clean, reconcile dry-run)' step has no specific proving command documented (e.g. exact CLI invocations + expected exit codes).
findings.suggested_edit: For the DIG dry-run, document the exact command and expected output pattern (e.g. 'rebar reconcile --dry-run exits 0 and prints N tickets read'). For E9 dogfood, list the three exact commands. Ticket closure can remain as-is with a note that it is a manual step.

findings.location: 'Why' section and E9: 'Exhaustive dogfood (3 interfaces, fsck clean, reconcile dry-run)'
findings.finding: The implied beneficiary is the rebar developer/operator who gains a single-implementation (Python-only) system, eliminating the bash engine maintenance burden. This is a well-understood internal-tooling cleanup; F4 ANTI-FP explicitly notes that an implied technical consumer counts for internal cleanup work. Dogfooding (3 interfaces, fsck, reconcile dry-run) serves as the validation mechanism. The value description is slightly thin — no explicit 'before/after' workflow comparison is articulated — but the dogfood criterion is a reasonable proxy.
findings.suggested_edit: Optionally add one sentence in 'Why' naming the concrete pain removed: e.g. 'Eliminates the need to maintain two independent implementations of ticket read/list/show logic.'

findings.location: Title vs Plan body
findings.finding: The title promises: delete bash dispatcher + engine (after E7e reconciler/validate fix), then E9 docs + close. The body faithfully delivers: E7e fixes the last two runtime consumers, E7-final deletes the engine artifacts, and E9 covers docs and ticket closure. No scope drift detected — files to KEEP are explicitly called out, files to DELETE are enumerated, and changed callers (reconciler, validate) are acknowledged.

findings.location: Entire plan: E7e + E7-final + E9 sections; AC list (6 ACs touching reconciler fix, mass deletion, docs, ticket closure)
findings.finding: This is a single ticket carrying three structurally independent goals joined by 'and': (E7e) a behavioral fix to the reconciler/validate CLI wiring; (E7-final) a mass deletion of ~50+ files across multiple layers (engine, shims, helpers, tests, imports); and (E9) documentation updates + ticket closure. It also has 6 ACs covering >3 layers (CLI entry-point, reconciler subprocess, engine helpers, test suite, docs, external tracker). The E7e behavioral fix (touching applier.py, invariants.py, reconcile.py, validate.py, __main__.py, engine_env) is itself a non-trivial multi-file change that could regress the reconciler — it is the highest-risk piece and is gated as a blocker, but it is bundled into the same ticket as the deletion. A failure in E7e requires re-running the entire ticket. The sequencing (E7e → E7-final → E9) is evidence-gated and vertically sliced within the ticket, which is good, but the three phases are independent enough that they would each be safer as separate, closeable commits/tickets.
findings.suggested_edit: Split into: (1) E7e ticket: add __main__.py, repoint REBAR_TICKET_CLI + _default_ticket_cmd, live dry-run green; (2) E7-final ticket: mass deletion + import cleanup + unit test update; (3) E9 ticket: docs + closure. If splitting is not desired, at minimum add a note that E7e must be merged and CI-green before the deletion commit is authored.

findings.location: AC: 'Zero src/rebar/_engine/*.sh; dispatcher + 9 helpers + 5 shims + ticket_txn deleted' vs plan body listing '10 helpers' (ticket-reducer.py, ticket-graph.py, ticket-reads.py, ticket-commands.py, ticket-delete-unlink-scan.py, ticket-alias-compute.py, ticket-alias-resolve.py, ticket-list-descendants.py, ticket-unblock.py, ticket-bridge-fsck.py)
findings.finding: Terminology drift / count mismatch: The E7-final body section lists 10 dispatcher-only helpers by name, but AC bullet 2 says 'dispatcher + 9 helpers'. The discrepancy makes the acceptance criterion ambiguous — the agent cannot be sure which count is authoritative. Additionally, the E9 work (docs/architecture.md, docs/bash-migration.md, dogfood, ticket closures 'adult-oxide-slave + nervy-hold-dip') is described in the body but covered by only a single thin AC bullet ('E9 docs updated; adult-oxide-slave + nervy-hold-dip closed'), which omits mention of the required dogfood run ('Exhaustive dogfood (3 interfaces, fsck clean, reconcile dry-run)'). No duplicate criteria found.

The plan specifies updating test_engine_dir.py to replace the dispatcher assertion, which is correct remove-test work. However, no test coverage is described for the newly introduced __main__.py entry point (the key new behavior in E7e).
