6. No rate-limiting or abuse-protection constraint is stated for the public /authorize and /_callback endpoints.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Specify cookie Max-Age (e.g. 8 h). 2. Pick Lambda@Edge vs Function URL explicitly. 3. State the Secrets Manager path convention and whether the secrets must be pre-created. 4. Specify HMAC algorithm (e.g. HMAC-SHA256). 5. Clarify the rejection logic: reject if hd claim != 'navapbc.com' OR email domain != 'navapbc.com' (both must pass). 6. Add a constraint note on rate-limiting.

findings.location: Acceptance criteria section (all three ACs) and Plan body
findings.finding:
No proving commands or verification steps are described anywhere in the plan:
1. AC1 ('full Google code flow works') has no end-to-end test described — no curl sequence, no integration test, no synthetic smoke test after deploy.
2. AC2 ('hd/email != navapbc.com is rejected'; 'return_to restricted') has no test case specifying how to exercise the rejection path (e.g. a test Google account outside navapbc.com, or a crafted return_to pointing to evil.com).
3. AC3 (cookie attributes) has no command to inspect Set-Cookie headers (e.g. curl -I, browser DevTools screenshot, automated cookie-attribute assertion).
4. The plan uses no hedge words, but equally provides no affirmative proving commands — 'works' and 'restricted' are asserted without a verification mechanism.
5. There is no mention of unit tests for the state-signing/verification logic or the hd/email check.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a 'Verification' section enumerating: (a) `curl -v 'https://auth.solutions.navateam.com/authorize?return_to=...'` to confirm redirect to Google with correct state; (b) a test callback with a non-navapbc.com ID token to confirm 403; (c) `curl -I` on a successful callback to assert Set-Cookie header attributes; (d) a crafted return_to=https://evil.com to confirm 400/redirect-blocked; (e) unit tests for HMAC state sign/verify.

findings.location: Plan body (no problem/persona statement); AC1–AC3
findings.finding: The plan names no specific user/stakeholder and articulates no problem being solved. Who is blocked today, and what can't they do without this auth host? The ACs are purely system-internal (endpoints exist, cookie attributes are set) with no before/after user workflow comparison or operational metric target. There is no dogfooding or validation mechanism described (e.g. 'a navapbc.com engineer can log in to app.solutions.navateam.com without a second login prompt').
findings.suggested_edit: Add a one-line problem statement, e.g.: 'navapbc.com engineers currently have no shared SSO layer for solutions.navateam.com apps; each app manages its own auth. This ticket provides a central OAuth host so apps can delegate authentication.' Then add a validation criterion: 'A navapbc.com user visiting a protected app is redirected to auth.solutions.navateam.com, authenticates once, and is returned to the app with a valid session cookie.'

findings.location: Title vs Plan body
Plan body: Terraform (infra/shared, us-east-1) … CloudFront + Lambda@Edge … Route53 … ACM … /authorize … /_callback … /_logout … Secrets Manager … HMAC-signed session cookie
findings.finding: This ticket spans at minimum: (1) Terraform infra layer (CloudFront distribution, Route53 records, ACM cert reference, Lambda packaging/deploy), (2) OAuth application layer (authorize URL construction, state signing, code exchange, ID-token verification), (3) cookie/session management layer (HMAC signing, SameSite/HttpOnly/Secure attributes, domain scoping), and (4) secrets management layer (Secrets Manager integration). That is ≥4 distinct layers and introduces a new public interface (/authorize, /_callback, /_logout). No thin vertical slice or evidence-gated MVP is described — the plan is a horizontal big-bang. A safer decomposition would be: (a) Terraform skeleton + health endpoint reachable; (b) OAuth happy-path with hardcoded navapbc.com check; (c) HMAC-signed cookie + rejection logic. The architectural fork (Lambda@Edge vs Function URL) also signals low scope-certainty.
findings.suggested_edit: Break into at least two child tickets: (1) 'Infra: CloudFront + Lambda stub + Route53/ACM — auth.solutions.navateam.com returns 200' (de-risks the infra layer first); (2) 'Auth logic: Google OAuth code flow, hd/email check, signed session cookie'. Resolve the Lambda@Edge-vs-Function-URL decision before starting.

findings.location: Plan body: 'Config (client_id, secret, signing key) baked from Secrets Manager at deploy'
findings.finding: The plan states secrets are 'baked … at deploy time', which contradicts a core purpose of Secrets Manager — dynamic retrieval at runtime to avoid embedding secrets in deployment artifacts. If secrets are baked into a Lambda deployment package or environment variable at deploy time, rotation in Secrets Manager has no effect until the next deploy, and the secret value lives in the artifact/config indefinitely. This is a cross-section contradiction: the infrastructure section mandates Secrets Manager (implying runtime fetch) while the phrasing 'baked at deploy' implies a static inject, sending the implementer in two different directions.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify the fetch model: either (a) Lambda reads from Secrets Manager at cold-start and caches with a TTL, or (b) secrets are injected as environment variables at deploy time via Terraform data sources. If (b), add a note that rotation requires re-deploy and that the value will appear in Terraform state.

findings.location: Acceptance criteria — all three ACs; Plan body: '/authorize, /_callback, /_logout'
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple new user-facing auth flows (OAuth code exchange, session cookie issuance, return_to redirect, hd/email rejection, logout) but provides zero testing plan. The three ACs are purely functional acceptance statements with no mention of how they will be verified: no unit tests for HMAC signing/verification, no integration or e2e test for the code flow, no negative-path tests for the hd != navapbc.com rejection (AC2), no test for open-redirect prevention (return_to validation), and no test for cookie attribute correctness. All of these are new user-facing security-critical flows where missing failure-path tests is a MAJOR finding under E5.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a testing section covering: (1) unit tests for state signing/verification and HMAC cookie logic; (2) happy-path e2e test for the full Google code flow against a stub IdP; (3) negative-path tests: non-navapbc.com hd → 403, invalid state → reject, return_to outside *.solutions.navateam.com → reject; (4) cookie attribute assertions (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Lax, Domain, Max-Age).

findings.location: Plan body: 'Lambda@Edge (or a Lambda Function URL fronted by CloudFront)'; '/_callback (exchange code, verify ID-token…)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces new I/O paths (Google OAuth token endpoint call in /_callback, Secrets Manager fetch at deploy/cold-start) with no latency targets, no timeout values on the Google token exchange call, and no statement about cold-start impact on Lambda@Edge. Lambda@Edge has strict 5-second (viewer-request) and 30-second (origin-request) limits; if the Google token endpoint is slow the callback will silently time out. Additionally, cost economics are unaddressed: Lambda@Edge charges per-request at every CloudFront edge PoP globally and cannot be confined to us-east-1, which may be undesirably expensive for an internal auth host.
findings.suggested_edit: State a latency budget for the /_callback token exchange (e.g. 3 s timeout on the Google call), confirm which Lambda@Edge event type is used and its limit, and evaluate whether a Lambda Function URL + CloudFront origin (not Lambda@Edge) is preferred for cost and regional control. Document expected RPS and estimated monthly cost.

findings.location: Plan body: '/_callback (exchange code, verify ID-token…)'; 'Config … baked from Secrets Manager at deploy'
findings.finding: Three reliability gaps: (1) The Google token endpoint call in /_callback has no stated retry/timeout/circuit-breaker policy — if Google is slow or returns a 5xx, the plan gives no degraded-mode behavior. (2) The Secrets Manager fetch ('baked at deploy') has no fallback if Secrets Manager is unavailable at deploy/cold-start. (3) There is no observability specification — no CloudWatch metrics, no structured error logging, no alerting on 403 spikes or callback failures — meaning operators cannot detect or debug auth outages.
findings.suggested_edit: Add: (a) explicit timeout (e.g. 3 s) and at-most-1-retry on the Google token call; (b) a startup-failure mode (Lambda init failure bubbles a 5xx rather than silently serving broken state); (c) CloudWatch metric/alarm for /_callback error rate and 403 count; (d) structured logging for auth failures (without logging tokens or secrets).

findings.location: Plan body: '/authorize (build the Google authorize URL with a signed state carrying return_to)'; 'hd + email end in navapbc.com'; 'HMAC-signed session cookie'; 'Config … baked from Secrets Manager at deploy'
findings.finding: Several security gaps: (1) ACCESS CLASSIFICATION (OWASP A01): /authorize and /_logout are public endpoints — that is fine — but their access level is never explicitly stated. /_callback must only be reachable after Google redirects to it; the plan doesn't address whether direct, forged requests to /_callback are rejected (CSRF via state param is mentioned implicitly via 'signed state' but PKCE or state binding to session is not specified). (2) LEAST PRIVILEGE: IAM role for the Lambda is not described; the plan doesn't confirm it is scoped only to the specific Secrets Manager secret(s) and nothing else. (3) SECRET LIFECYCLE: 'baked from Secrets Manager at deploy' is ambiguous — if this means the Terraform data source reads the secret and injects it as a plaintext Lambda env var, the secret appears in Terraform state (potentially in an S3 backend without additional encryption) and in CloudWatch Logs if the Lambda logs its environment. (4) DATA PROTECTION: The plan calls for an HMAC-signed cookie but doesn't specify the signing algorithm (HS256? HS512?), key length, or cookie lifetime, making the 'bounded lifetime' AC unverifiable.
findings.suggested_edit: (1) Explicitly declare access level for each endpoint. (2) Specify that /_callback validates the state parameter is bound to the originating request (nonce in signed state or server-side state store) to prevent CSRF. (3) Confirm Lambda IAM role grants only secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on the specific secret ARN(s). (4) Specify that secrets are fetched at runtime (not baked into artifact), and that the Terraform state backend is encrypted. (5) Specify cookie Max-Age (e.g. 8 h), signing algorithm (e.g. HMAC-SHA256), and minimum key length (256-bit).

findings.location: Plan description: 'Terraform (infra/shared, us-east-1)... CloudFront + Lambda@Edge... Route53... reusing the *.solutions.navateam.com wildcard ACM cert. Config (client_id, secret, signing key) baked from Secrets Manager at deploy.'
findings.finding: (a) coupling_risk: The plan introduces cross-component dependencies — Lambda@Edge writing a cookie scoped to .solutions.navateam.com affects ALL existing subdomain apps (they will now receive and must validate this cookie), and CloudFront/Route53/Secrets Manager integrations are silently introduced with no acknowledgment of blast radius or interface contract for cookie consumers. (b) changeability: The hd/email domain check (navapbc.com) and cookie parameters (lifetime, SameSite policy) are described as implementation details with no mention of making them configurable (e.g., via SSM/Secrets Manager parameter or Terraform variable), creating a future hardcoded-change risk. (c) documentation: This is a novel architectural decision — a central auth host serving as the SSO gateway for all *.solutions.navateam.com apps — with no mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design doc capturing the auth pattern, cookie contract, or session validation expectations for downstream consumers.
findings.suggested_edit: 1. Add an ADR or design-doc section capturing: the auth host pattern, the cookie contract (name, signing algorithm, lifetime, validation instructions for consumers), and the cross-component coupling to all *.solutions.navateam.com apps. 2. Make the allowed domain (navapbc.com), cookie lifetime, and signing key rotation policy configurable via Terraform variables or SSM parameters rather than hardcoded. 3. Explicitly acknowledge which downstream apps are consumers of the cookie and how they will validate it.

findings.location: Plan description: 'Lambda@Edge (or a Lambda Function URL fronted by CloudFront)' and 'Config (client_id, secret, signing key) baked from Secrets Manager at deploy'
findings.finding: Two high-uncertainty architectural choices are asserted without empirical validation: (1) 'Lambda@Edge OR a Lambda Function URL fronted by CloudFront' — this is an unresolved design fork with meaningful behavioral differences (cold start latency, Secrets Manager access patterns, deployment region constraints, cookie-manipulation capabilities differ between the two). The plan asserts it can work either way without a spike to confirm. (2) 'Config baked from Secrets Manager at deploy' — Lambda@Edge has well-known constraints around environment variables and Secrets Manager access at edge (execution context, IAM role propagation, cold-start secret fetching) that are not trivially composable; this pattern is novel enough to warrant a probe before full build-out.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a time-boxed spike (1–2 days) to: (a) resolve the Lambda@Edge vs. Function URL choice by prototyping Secrets Manager access and cookie manipulation in the actual chosen runtime, and (b) verify the 'baked at deploy' secret pattern works within Lambda@Edge's execution environment constraints. Document the spike outcome as the basis for the final architecture choice.

findings.location: Plan description overall
Plan description: 'Reject non-navapbc with a clear 403 page'
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least one user-facing surface: a '403 page' for rejected users. The plan does not address WCAG 2.1 AA compliance, keyboard navigability, screen-reader semantics, or color-contrast for this page. However, if this is a minimal static error page (plain HTML with no interactive controls), the a11y surface is very limited. The plan does not clarify whether the 403 page is purely static/informational or interactive, making it ambiguous whether WCAG AA done-definitions are required.
findings.suggested_edit: Clarify whether the 403 page is a static, non-interactive HTML response. If so, ensure it uses semantic HTML (proper heading structure, sufficient contrast) and note that in the AC. If it contains interactive elements (e.g., a 'Try again' link), add explicit WCAG 2.1 AA done-definitions to the acceptance criteria.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: all three ACs cover only success paths; Plan description for /_callback: 'exchange code, verify ID-token hd + email... set the HMAC-signed session cookie... redirect to validated return_to'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a user-facing OAuth flow with multiple high-stakes non-happy-path scenarios that are unaddressed: (a) What happens if the Google OAuth callback returns an error (e.g., user denies consent, or Google returns error=access_denied)? (b) What happens on state mismatch / CSRF detection — is there a meaningful error page or silent failure? (c) What happens if the authorization code exchange fails (network timeout, invalid grant)? (d) What is the session cookie expiry behavior — does the user see a re-auth prompt or a silent 401? (e) The /_logout flow has no AC covering abandon/error exit (e.g., logout with no valid cookie). Only the happy path (navapbc.com user authenticates successfully) and the domain-rejection 403 are addressed.
findings.suggested_edit: Add non-happy-path acceptance criteria covering: (1) Google OAuth error response → user sees a clear error page, not a 500/blank. (2) State mismatch → CSRF rejection with a distinct error message. (3) Code exchange timeout/failure → user sees a retry-able error page. (4) Expired/invalid session cookie → user is redirected to /authorize rather than receiving a raw 401. (5) /_logout with no cookie → graceful no-op with redirect.

findings.location: Plan description overall; no ADR/doc section mentioned
findings.finding: (a) new_needed: The plan introduces a novel central-auth architectural pattern (SSO gateway via cookie for all *.solutions.navateam.com subdomains), a new Terraform module (infra/shared), a new cookie contract (name, HMAC algorithm, payload schema, validation instructions for consumers), and a new Google OAuth integration point. None of these are accompanied by a planned ADR, CLAUDE.md update, runbook, or design doc. Downstream app teams need the cookie contract documented to implement validation. (b) invalidated: No mention of whether existing docs/READMEs for infra/shared or *.solutions.navateam.com apps reference auth patterns that will now be superseded. (c) navigable: N/A — no doc is planned at all.
findings.suggested_edit: Add to the plan: (1) An ADR (or equivalent design doc) capturing the central-auth pattern, the rationale for Lambda@Edge/CloudFront, the cookie contract (field names, signing algorithm, lifetime, validation pseudocode), and the Google OAuth integration constraints. (2) A CLAUDE.md or infra README update for the new infra/shared Terraform module. (3) A check/update of any existing auth-related docs for *.solutions.navateam.com apps to prevent stale references.

findings.location: Plan: 'Config (client_id, secret, signing key) baked from Secrets Manager at deploy' and 'HMAC-signed session cookie Domain=.solutions.navateam.com'
findings.finding:
Multiple shared-state elements lack fully defined lifecycle coverage:

(create) Secrets Manager secrets (client_id, OAuth secret, HMAC signing key) — who creates them and when (first Terraform apply? manual pre-seeding? a bootstrap script?) is not specified. The plan says they are 'baked at deploy' but not who provisions them initially.

(update) The HMAC signing key is shared state consumed by every Lambda@Edge instance across all CloudFront PoPs. Key rotation is unaddressed: who rotates it, how in-flight sessions signed with the old key are handled during a key rotation, and whether rotation is atomic across all edge nodes is not defined. A naive key swap would instantly invalidate all live sessions.

(consume) The session cookie is consumed by downstream apps on *.solutions.navateam.com. Those consumers are not enumerated, and their tolerance for absent/stale/invalidated cookies (e.g., after key rotation) is not described.

(retire) There is no retirement path for the signing key, old Secrets Manager versions, or for session cookies that become orphaned if the auth host is decommissioned.

(concurrency_safe) The /_callback endpoint performs a code-exchange (check-then-act): a replayed or concurrent OAuth callback with the same code could result in a race. The plan does not specify any nonce/state deduplication store or idempotency mechanism to prevent double-redemption of the authorization code.
findings.suggested_edit: Add: (1) A bootstrap step (e.g., `terraform apply` pre-condition or a documented manual step) that provisions secrets before first deploy. (2) A key-rotation procedure that stages the new signing key alongside the old one (dual-verify, single-sign) for at least one session TTL before retiring the old key. (3) Enumerate downstream consumers of the session cookie and document their fallback on cookie absence (redirect to /authorize). (4) Specify a state/nonce cache (e.g., DynamoDB or ElastiCache with TTL) to deduplicate /_callback invocations for the same authorization code, making the exchange idempotent.

findings.location: Plan description (entire deployment section) and all three Acceptance Criteria
findings.finding:
This plan introduces a brand-new auth host that will gate access for all *.solutions.navateam.com traffic. No staged rollout, rollback, or deploy-ordering strategy is defined:

(staged_rollout) The plan describes a single Terraform apply that immediately stands up CloudFront + Lambda@Edge + Route53 live DNS for auth.solutions.navateam.com with 100% traffic. There is no mention of a feature flag, weighted routing, canary Lambda version, or blue/green CloudFront distribution to limit blast radius during initial rollout.

(rollback) No rollback path is specified. Rolling back a Lambda@Edge deployment is non-trivial (replicas propagate to all CloudFront PoPs and cannot be deleted until disassociated; propagation takes minutes). The plan does not describe how to quickly revert to a pre-auth state if the new flow breaks user access.

(deploy_ordering) Downstream apps on *.solutions.navateam.com presumably need to be updated to redirect unauthenticated users to auth.solutions.navateam.com/authorize. The plan does not specify whether the auth host must be fully live before any consumer is updated, nor how to handle the window where the auth host is live but consumers are not yet integrated (or vice versa).
findings.suggested_edit: Add: (1) A staged rollout note — e.g., stand up the CloudFront distribution with a test/staging DNS alias first (auth-staging.solutions.navateam.com), validate the full OAuth flow, then cut over the production DNS. (2) An explicit rollback step: document how to quickly disable the Lambda@Edge association and restore any prior DNS/access state, including the CloudFront propagation delay caveat. (3) A deploy-ordering section: the auth host must be fully live and validated before any downstream app is reconfigured to redirect to it.

findings.location: AC: Lever 1 ('a documented mechanism... with a written argument (or a run)'), AC: Lever 3 ('are written up as candidate optimizations'), AC: Lever 2 ('proven diagnostic-only')
findings.finding:
Multiple criteria fail the observable-outcome and in-session-evaluable checks:

(a) Lever 1 AC accepts 'a written argument OR a run' — a written argument is effort/documentation, not an observable system outcome. No concrete command or artifact proves cross-state isolation holds.

(b) Lever 3 AC is explicitly effort-framed ('written up as candidate optimizations') with no observable system change — it is documentation busywork, not a durable end-state the system can be in.

(c) Lever 2's 'proven diagnostic-only (captured content, determinism hashes, and verdicts byte-identical with the flag on vs off)' is evaluable, but only if `--timing` is actually implemented; the AC bundles implementation with a diff-based proof, making it right-sized. However, the 'byte-identical' proof requires running a full ~4h conformance sweep twice — this is NOT in-session completable in one working session.

(d) The Lever 1 AC also requires 'a run showing cross-state isolation does not perturb content-signature.json or k>=3 determinism controls' — each such run is ~4h × 3 states; two baseline+parallel runs could be ~24h, far outside a single session.

These together mean several criteria are either effort-framed, post-session-only validatable, or lack a specific observable system outcome.
findings.suggested_edit: Rephrase Lever 1 AC to require a specific artifact (e.g., 'a CI job definition or orchestration script that launches N isolated environments in parallel, with a passing smoke-test diff of content-signature.json outputs'). Replace Lever 3 AC with a concrete deliverable (e.g., 'a written design doc committed to the repo at docs/optimizations/seed-trade-off.md with explicit fidelity-trade flag'). For Lever 2, scope the byte-identical proof to a short/single-page fixture run rather than a full 4h sweep, and call out the fixture explicitly.

findings.location: AC: Lever 1 ('a documented mechanism'), AC: Lever 2 ('--timing (or equivalent)'), AC: Lever 3 ('written up as candidate optimizations, with the seed change explicitly flagged')
findings.finding:
Several scope boundaries are undefined or deferring real decisions to the executor:

1. 'Isolated environment' is never defined: does it mean separate VMs, Docker containers, separate CI jobs, separate machines? An executing agent cannot pick without stopping to ask. This is a blocking ambiguity for Lever 1.

2. '--timing (or equivalent)' — the 'or equivalent' punt leaves the interface entirely open. What flag name, what output format (JSON/text/log), where written (stdout/file), what granularity ('per-phase/per-step' is stated but 'phase' and 'step' are not defined relative to the codebase).

3. Lever 3 says the seed question is 'a design decision, not a free win' and must be 'recorded' — but the AC says only 'written up.' There is no spec for where, what format, what decisions must be made vs. deferred, or what 'decision recorded' means as a verifiable artifact.

4. 'Adoption gate is enforced for ANY parallel config' — no spec for how enforcement is implemented (CI check? runtime guard? documentation?), leaving the mechanism entirely to the executor.
findings.suggested_edit: Define 'isolated environment' precisely (e.g., 'separate Docker Compose stacks on separate CI runners'). Specify --timing output format and location. Define 'phase' and 'step' in relation to the existing codebase structure. Replace 'or equivalent' with a committed interface contract. Specify that 'adoption gate enforcement' means a runtime guard in the harness (not just a written policy).

findings.location: AC: all five criteria; PLAN body has no 'steps' section
findings.finding:
The plan contains NO implementation steps — only a goals/analysis section and acceptance criteria. There is no step-to-AC mapping and no proving commands:

(a) No step describes HOW the isolated-environment orchestration will be built, so no AC can be mapped to a step.
(b) No proving command exists for Lever 1's isolation guarantee — 'a written argument' is not a command that produces evidence.
(c) The `--timing` AC requires byte-identical output comparison but names no command (e.g., `diff`, hash check command, specific test target) to produce that evidence.
(d) Lever 3's AC is 'written up' — no step describes who writes it, in what format, or how a reviewer confirms it exists.
(e) The invariants AC ('All three invariants preserved') has no associated step or proving command whatsoever.

The entire plan is analysis/rationale with acceptance criteria bolted on, but zero described implementation steps — meaning the union of steps cannot reach the end-state because there are no steps.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit 'Implementation steps' section with: (1) steps for Lever 1 orchestration, (2) steps for --timing flag implementation + the specific diff/hash command that proves byte-identity, (3) steps for writing and committing the Lever 3 design doc, (4) a CI command that acts as the adoption gate. Map each AC to its step(s).

findings.location: CONTEXT section: 'A full conformance sweep is ~4h per state; the question was whether parallelism can cut that.' BOTTOM LINE: '~3x faster, reliability-neutral/positive'
Title vs. Plan body
findings.finding: The title promises 'cross-state isolated-environment parallelization (~3x, safe) + --timing diagnostic + per-request infra/seed levers — audit-run wall-clock reduction.' The body faithfully delivers analysis and acceptance criteria for exactly these three levers, including explicit warnings about what NOT to do (intra-host parallelism), and the invariants section ensures no contradictory behavior change. The scope matches the title without material drift.

findings.location: Plan body: three independent levers described; AC has 5 criteria spanning orchestration infra, diagnostic tooling, design documentation, and runtime gate enforcement
findings.finding:
The ticket bundles three structurally independent goals (Lever 1: infrastructure orchestration change; Lever 2: diagnostic instrumentation; Lever 3: design documentation) joined by a structural 'and.' These are independent in implementation, risk profile, and validatability:

- Lever 1 requires infrastructure/orchestration work (new CI jobs, environment isolation).
- Lever 2 requires code instrumentation in the harness.
- Lever 3 requires design documentation.

Each has distinct failure modes and could be completed independently. The ticket has >6 sub-deliverables if the 5 ACs are unpacked. There is no vertical-slice sequencing or MVP — the plan does not suggest implementing the lowest-risk item (e.g., --timing flag) first to gain data before committing to Lever 1 infrastructure. There are no child tickets despite epic-scale scope. Sizing signals: touches ≥3 layers (infra/CI, harness code, documentation), introduces a new interface (--timing flag, parallel orchestration mechanism), and has low scope-certainty (Lever 3 is explicitly deferred investigation).
findings.suggested_edit: Split into three child tickets: (1) --timing flag implementation (Lever 2, lowest risk, enables data for Lever 1 decision), (2) cross-state isolated-environment orchestration (Lever 1, gated on Lever 2 data), (3) Lever 3 design doc (can proceed in parallel with 1). Sequence: Lever 2 first as the evidence-gated MVP, since timing data informs whether Lever 1's ~3x is the right investment.

findings.location: Plan body: 'LEVER 2 — --timing diagnostic'; AC: 'proven diagnostic-only (captured content, determinism hashes, and verdicts byte-identical with the flag on vs off)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces new behavior (--timing flag, cross-state parallelization mechanism, adoption-gate enforcement) that is in principle testable. However, the plan is explicitly framed as an optimization/infrastructure ticket (P3 deferred), and the primary 'test' is the adoption gate itself — byte-identical content-signature.json + k>=3 determinism controls vs. serial baseline — which is a well-defined behavioral oracle, not a self-authored snapshot. The diagnostic-only proof for --timing (flag-on vs flag-off produces byte-identical output) is a valid, non-tautological verification. No new user-facing flow in the traditional sense exists, so missing failure/boundary paths are not applicable. Thin-but-present coverage is PASS per criterion rules.

findings.location: LEVER 1: 'Run each state's conformance on its OWN isolated environment (separate host/CPU + its own dev server + its own DB)'; LEVER 3: 'O(N^2)->O(N) seed question'
findings.finding: The plan explicitly discusses performance (wall-clock reduction, O(N^2) vs O(N) complexity, ~3x speedup estimate) and introduces new I/O and compute patterns (isolated-environment parallelization, per-phase timing instrumentation). The overlay applies. The plan gives a directional throughput goal (~3x for cross-state) but provides no time-bounded done-definition for the hot path (e.g., 'a 3-state sweep must complete within X hours under isolated parallelism'). The O(N^2)->O(N) seed trade-off is correctly identified as a fidelity decision and explicitly deferred, which is acceptable. The ~3x estimate is stated as a cap ('caps the safe win at ~3x') but is not backed by a measurement or baseline figure. This is advisory (minor) since the ticket is explicitly P3/deferred and the primary deliverable is a 'documented mechanism' and 'written argument', not a shipped production optimization.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a concrete baseline figure (e.g., 'current 3-state sweep = ~12h wall-clock; target under Lever 1 = ~4h') and a minimum acceptance threshold so the implementer and reviewer can verify the ~3x claim rather than treat it as aspirational.

findings.location: LEVER 1 isolation argument; AC: 'cross-state isolation does not share CPU/server/DB'
findings.finding: The plan adds no external integrations, file writes with mutation risk, or stateful transitions beyond what already exists in the harness. The primary reliability concern — that concurrent load would corrupt determinism/arrival gates — is explicitly addressed and mitigated by the isolation requirement. The adoption gate (byte-identical content-signature + k>=3 determinism) serves as a correctness/reliability circuit-breaker. No new failure points that lack handling are introduced by the plan's described scope. Observability is partially addressed via the --timing diagnostic (Lever 2). The plan is not applicable for the full T5b overlay (no new writes, no new external deps).

The plan introduces no new endpoints, data storage, PII handling, auth boundaries, credentials, roles, or grants. The isolated-environment parallelization is an infrastructure execution model change, not a new access surface. T5c is not applicable.

findings.location: LEVER 1 — cross-state isolated-environment parallelization; INVARIANTS section; Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC2
findings.finding: (a) coupling_risk — PASS: the plan explicitly isolates state environments (separate host/CPU/dev server/DB), so new cross-component dependencies are acknowledged and justified rather than silently introduced. The anti-coupling argument is central to the plan. (b) changeability — MINOR: the '~3x' speedup factor and the 'k>=3 determinism gate' threshold are stated as fixed constants with no mention of being configurable. The plan notes the factor is 'capped at the x-states factor (~3x for the current 3-state set)' — if the state count changes, that cap changes, and there is no mention of this being data-driven or configurable. (c) documentation — AMBIGUOUS: the plan introduces a novel orchestration pattern (cross-state isolated-environment parallelization with an adoption gate) and references HERMETIC/PORTABLE/REPEATABLE invariants, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is called out anywhere in the plan or acceptance criteria. The adoption-gate contract (byte-identical content-signature.json + k>=3 gate as the arbiter) is a significant architectural decision that should be captured formally.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC: 'An ADR or CLAUDE.md section documents (a) the cross-state parallelization architecture, (b) the adoption-gate contract and its arbiter (drift sentinel / determinism gate), and (c) the explicit prohibition on intra-host parallelism without empirical clearance.' Also make the state-count factor dynamically derived rather than a hardcoded narrative constant.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1–AC5; THE ADOPTION GATE section; LEVER 2
findings.finding: The plan is complex/novel (novel orchestration pattern, unverified performance claims, a k>=3 determinism gate that could be perturbed). However, the plan already includes empirical validation steps: (1) AC1 requires 'a written argument OR a run showing cross-state isolation does not perturb content-signature.json or k>=3 determinism controls'; (2) the adoption gate explicitly requires any parallel config to reproduce the serial baseline byte-identically before use in a scored run; (3) Lever 2 introduces a `--timing` diagnostic specifically to derive where the ~4h goes (measurement-first, not assertion-first); (4) the seed/O(N^2) item in Lever 3 is flagged as 'a fidelity trade — decision recorded, not silently adopted.' These are sufficient validation-present signals.

findings.location: CONTEXT section; INVARIANTS; Acceptance Criteria AC5
findings.finding: The plan is purely additive: it introduces new execution modes (parallel, --timing) and a write-up, but explicitly states 'no behavior is broken, and nothing here changes for an in-flight run — switching execution model mid-run would break hermeticity.' The REPEATABLE invariant and adoption gate ensure the serial baseline is preserved. No existing interface, schema, or data shape is changed or removed. No rollback path is needed because the change is non-destructive; the adoption gate itself acts as the compatibility guard. The seed/O(N^2) item is explicitly flagged as a fidelity trade requiring a deliberate design decision, not a silent change.

findings.location: Entire plan
findings.finding: Not applicable. The plan introduces no new user-facing UI. All changes are infrastructure/orchestration/CLI diagnostic (--timing flag). No WCAG or inclusive-UX checks are warranted.

findings.location: Entire plan
findings.finding: Not applicable. The plan introduces no user-facing interaction surface. The --timing flag is a developer diagnostic CLI switch; there is no user flow, form, or interactive UI to review.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs); LEVER 1 — adoption gate; INVARIANTS section
findings.finding: (a) new_needed — The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern: cross-state isolated-environment parallelization with a formal adoption-gate contract (byte-identical content-signature.json + k>=3 determinism gate as arbiter, explicit prohibition on intra-host parallelism without empirical clearance). This is exactly the kind of decision that warrants an ADR or CLAUDE.md update. None of the five ACs requires producing such a document; AC1 requires only 'a documented mechanism' and 'a written argument,' but neither is tied to a specific artifact type (ADR, CLAUDE.md, design doc) or location. (b) invalidated — The plan references an existing harness, adapter, coverage-map, scenario data, and existing invariants (HERMETIC/PORTABLE/REPEATABLE). It is unclear whether any existing documentation describes the serial-only execution model in a way that would become stale once parallelization is introduced. (c) navigable — Not a concern given plan length.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC: 'An ADR or named CLAUDE.md / design-doc section is created (or updated) capturing: (1) the cross-state isolated parallelization architecture; (2) the adoption-gate contract and its arbiter; (3) the explicit prohibition on intra-host parallelism without empirical clearance; (4) the HERMETIC/PORTABLE/REPEATABLE invariants as standing constraints for future work.' Also audit existing docs that describe the serial execution model and add an update step so those references remain accurate.

findings.location: N/A — plan is a diagnostic/parallelization optimization for a CLI/batch harness
N/A — plan is a CLI/batch audit harness, not a deployed or long-running service

AC bullet 1: 'Coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` (and/or `path`) signal, re-recorded without changing the frozen determinism baseline (or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented).'
findings.finding: The '(and/or `path`)' hedge leaves it genuinely unclear which fields will exist in the re-recorded coverage map. This makes the AC itself ambiguous about what observable state to check — is 'advanced' sufficient, is 'path' required, or are both required? The parenthetical 're-frozen + documented' branch is also a one-time transition phrasing ('re-frozen'), not a durable end-state check. However, the remaining ACs (unit tests that pass/fail, coupling-lint PASS) are concrete and in-session evaluable. The right-sizing is also slightly large — three distinct deliverables (coverage-map re-record, navigate.mjs re-drive logic, gather.mjs retry) are bundled — but each has a named test artifact, so they are individually verifiable within a session.
findings.suggested_edit: Replace 'per-step `advanced` (and/or `path`)' with a specific decision: e.g. 'per-step `advanced: boolean` field'. Rewrite the re-freeze branch as a durable check: 'If the baseline is re-frozen, the new frozen hash is committed and CI verifies against it.'

findings.location: AC bullet 1: 'Coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` (and/or `path`) signal'; AC bullet 2: 're-drives a single non-advancing step bounded'
findings.finding: Two genuine placeholder decisions are deferred to the executor: (1) 'advanced (and/or path)' — the executor must choose which field(s) to add to the schema without guidance; this is a real design decision, not an obvious convention. (2) 're-drives a single non-advancing step bounded' — no bound value is specified (bounded by what? a retry count? a timeout? both?). An agent cannot implement a retry loop without knowing the bound. Additionally, the re-recording process ('LLM-driven exploration') is described in the context/background but has no executable spec in the ACs — what triggers it, what environment, what command?
findings.suggested_edit: Specify the field choice explicitly (e.g. 'add `advanced: boolean` to each step entry'). Specify the re-drive bound (e.g. 'at most 1 re-drive per step, i.e. exactly one retry'). Add a concrete step or AC covering how re-recording is triggered (e.g. 'run `node scripts/record-coverage.mjs` in a headful browser session').

findings.location: AC bullet 1 (coverage-map re-record); AC bullet 3 ('Pinned by a test'); AC bullet 4 ('coupling-lint PASS')
findings.finding: Three claims lack a concrete proving command: (1) 'coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` flag' — no command to inspect or assert the schema of the recorded file is named (e.g. a JSON schema validation or a grep). (2) 'a genuinely unreachable surface still WARNs/STOPs loudly' in gather.mjs — AC bullet 3 says 'pinned by a test' but provides no test name, file, or describe-block anchor; the proving command is 'run a named test', not a named test. (3) 'HERMETIC/PORTABLE/REPEATABLE preserved; coupling-lint PASS' — coupling-lint is referenced but no command (e.g. `npm run lint:coupling`) is given. The re-recording step is described as 'LLM-driven exploration' with no deterministic proving command for its output.
findings.suggested_edit: For AC1, add: 'verified by `node scripts/validate-coverage-schema.mjs` (or equivalent JSON-schema check) that every step object contains `advanced: boolean`.' For AC3, name the test file and describe-block. For AC4, name the lint command.

findings.location: SCOPE section, bullet 1 ('reachByTrace step-level non-advance re-drive'); AC bullet 2 ('re-drives a single non-advancing step bounded, gated on the trace's `advanced` flag')
findings.finding: The plan body (SCOPE bullet 1) describes the root cause in detail — 17 identical nth=0 'Save and continue' steps, silent no-op, next step resolves on un-advanced page — but no AC explicitly covers the 'repeated-name swallow' scenario described in AC bullet 2 as 'repeated-name swallow prevented'. The AC bullet says it is 'pinned by a new navigate-level unit test (fake page)' covering three cases, which is good, but the third case ('genuine non-advance STILL STOPs') uses the hedge 'still returns a LOUD drifted/non-arrival' without specifying what observable signal constitutes 'LOUD' (throw? warn log? specific error code?). This creates a terminology gap between 'drifted/non-arrival' in AC2 and 'WARNs/STOPs' in AC3. Additionally, the plan body mentions 'path' as a candidate field but no AC measures whether 'path' is or is not included — leaving an uncovered deliverable branch.
findings.suggested_edit: Align terminology: use a single term (e.g. 'throws a DriftError / returns non-arrival status') consistently in AC2 and AC3. Resolve the 'and/or path' branch: either add a covering AC for 'path' or drop it from the plan body.

findings.location: CONTEXT ('re-freezing the determinism baseline'); AC bullet 1 ('re-recorded without changing the frozen determinism baseline (or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented)')
findings.finding: The CONTEXT section states that re-recording 'requires RE-RECORDING the coverage map... and re-freezing the determinism baseline', implying re-freezing is necessary. AC bullet 1 then presents two mutually exclusive branches: either re-record WITHOUT changing the baseline, OR re-freeze WITH documentation. These contradict each other at a cross-section level: the context says re-freezing is required (adding a field to every step necessarily changes the coverage map), while the AC allows a 'no change' path. An implementer reading the context would re-freeze; one reading only the AC might attempt to preserve the existing baseline — two incompatible directions. This is a genuine cross-section contradiction, not a within-section nitpick.
findings.suggested_edit: Reconcile: since adding `advanced` per step must change the recorded file, AC1 should read 'Coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` flag; the determinism baseline is re-frozen and the new frozen hash is committed and documented.' Remove the 'without changing' branch.

findings.location: Title vs SCOPE bullets 1 & 2; AC bullets 1–4
SCOPE section (items 1 and 2) and Acceptance Criteria (4 ACs)
findings.finding: The ticket has a structural 'and' joining two independent goals: (1) a coverage-map schema change + re-recording + reachByTrace re-drive logic, and (2) a gather.mjs arrival-retry mirror. These are separable concerns touching different files/layers (coverage-map serialization layer, navigate.mjs replay layer, gather.mjs orchestration layer — ≥3 layers, ≥3 files). The plan also carries a prerequisite re-recording step (LLM-driven exploration + baseline re-freeze) that is itself a mini-project; this is the riskiest piece and is not sequenced as a thin vertical slice before the logic changes. The ticket is leaf-shaped (no children) but epic-sized in practice: it requires schema migration of a persisted artifact, determinism-baseline re-freeze, two separate runtime behavior changes across two distinct modules, and a 4th AC for portability/lint. No evidence-gated sequencing (e.g. 'first add and verify the `advanced` flag in the coverage map, then build the re-drive logic against real recordings') is proposed — all four pieces land together, which is a horizontal big-bang. YAGNI: the new `arrivalRetryFor` abstraction is shared between conformance and gather (≥2 call sites mentioned) but the plan does not confirm a third real call-site, making it borderline.
findings.suggested_edit: Decompose into two sequenced child tickets: (A) 'Add `advanced`/`path` to coverage-map trace, re-record, re-freeze baseline' (schema + re-recording only, unblocks everything else), then (B) 'reachByTrace step-level non-advance re-drive + gather.mjs arrival-retry' (consumes the new flag, adds the retry logic). This makes the re-recording the de-risking MVP that must pass before any logic changes land.

findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC-2 and AC-3
SCOPE item 2 — 'depth-aware fresh-context arrival-retry (arrivalRetryFor)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces new retry I/O (fresh-context browser sessions per retry attempt in gather.mjs, mirroring conformance's arrivalRetryFor). The conformance gate was tuned to ≥6 attempts for non-idempotent surfaces, but the plan gives no done-definition or latency budget for gather's retry loop, no upper bound on total additional page navigations per gather run, and no statement of whether gather runs are time-bounded end-to-end. Whether this is a real latency/cost concern depends on how many deep surfaces gather visits and how often retries fire — unknowable from the plan text alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit upper bound on total retry attempts per gather run and a wall-clock timeout or step-count cap so a pathological retry storm does not silently make gather runs unbounded.

findings.location: SCOPE items 1–2 and AC-2, AC-3
findings.finding: The plan explicitly addresses the loud-STOP guarantee for permanently-stuck steps (AC-2: 'genuine non-advance STILL STOPs') and the WARN/STOP for genuinely unreachable surfaces in gather (AC-3). The non-advance re-drive is bounded (single re-drive), preventing infinite retry. Coverage-map writes are described as a re-recording step (not an incremental write), so partial-state corruption is not a concern. Error states are surfaced, not swallowed.

findings.location: Entire plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces no new endpoints, auth boundaries, PII handling, or credential grants. It is internal automation tooling (browser replay + gather). Security overlay is not applicable.

findings.location: SCOPE item 1 — 'requires RE-RECORDING the coverage map … and re-freezing the determinism baseline'; AC-1
findings.finding: The plan introduces a schema change to a persisted artifact (coverage-map trace, adding `advanced`/`path` per step) and a deliberate re-freeze of the determinism baseline. This is a cross-component coupling change (navigate.mjs reachStep writer ↔ reachByTrace reader ↔ frozen coverage-map file format). No ADR or design-doc update is called out beyond a parenthetical 'or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented'. The existing ADR-0057 covered the arrival-retry; this ticket extends the persisted schema — that is a new architectural decision (schema versioning, portability constraint, re-recording protocol) that warrants a named ADR or CLAUDE.md update. Without it, future maintainers lack a record of why `advanced` was added, what re-recording means, and how to evolve the schema again. The coupling-lint pass mentioned in AC-4 is good but does not substitute for documentation of the schema decision.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly add to the Acceptance Criteria: 'A new ADR (or ADR-0057 amendment) documents the coverage-map schema extension (`advanced`/`path`), the re-recording protocol, and the portability constraint (gate on trace flag, never a label/prototype literal).'

findings.location: AC1: 'Coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` (and/or `path`) signal, re-recorded without changing the frozen determinism baseline (or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented).'
findings.finding: Two high-uncertainty, unvalidated assumptions are asserted without a spike or probe: (1) that re-recording the coverage map will NOT change the frozen determinism baseline (or that re-freezing it is safe/low-cost). The plan acknowledges the risk ('or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented') but provides no empirical validation step — no fixture, no pilot run, no measurement of baseline drift before committing to the full re-record. (2) That a single-step in-context re-drive is reliably cheaper than abandoning ~50 steps — this is asserted ('cheaper than abandoning ~50 steps') but no benchmark or probe is planned to confirm it under realistic stochastic conditions. Both are correctness/cost choices that cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit spike/probe AC: 'Before full implementation, run a pilot re-recording on a representative subset of traces, measure determinism-baseline delta, and confirm single-step re-drive success rate under simulated dropped-step conditions. Gate the full build-out on spike results.' This de-risks both the re-recording assumption and the re-drive-vs-abandon trade-off before the agent invests in full implementation.

findings.location: AC1: 're-recorded without changing the frozen determinism baseline (or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented)'; SCOPE section: 'requires RE-RECORDING the coverage map … and re-freezing the determinism baseline'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a breaking schema change to the persisted coverage-map trace format (adding `advanced` and/or `path` per step). This is a destructive/irreversible change to a shared data artifact that existing consumers (conformance gate, gather.mjs, any tooling reading the coverage map) rely on. The plan acknowledges the re-recording need but provides NO rollback path, no expand-contract sequence (e.g., write new fields alongside old, allow consumers to tolerate absence, then remove old format), and no explicit migration strategy. The parenthetical '(or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented)' is acknowledgement only — it is not a reversibility mechanism. If the re-recorded map introduces regression or the baseline drift is unacceptable, there is no stated back-out path.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit expand-contract sequence: (1) add `advanced`/`path` as optional fields to the trace schema while keeping existing fields unchanged so current consumers still work; (2) re-record and validate; (3) only then update consumers to require the new fields. Also add a rollback note: 'If re-recorded baseline diverges unacceptably, restore from the previous frozen snapshot at <ref>.' Document the migration in an ADR update.

findings.location: Entire plan
Entire plan

SCOPE section: 'Must stay PORTABLE (gate on the trace's own advanced flag, never a label/prototype literal)'; AC1: 're-recorded without changing the frozen determinism baseline (or with the baseline deliberately re-frozen + documented)'
findings.finding: The plan introduces: (a) a new per-step schema contract for coverage-map traces (new `advanced`/`path` fields); (b) a new behavioral pattern in reachByTrace (single-step non-advance re-drive); (c) a new arrival-retry pattern mirrored into gather.mjs. These are all architectural decisions that extend or modify existing contracts. The plan references ADR-0057 as prior art but does NOT specify that a new ADR update (or addendum) will be produced to document the new schema contract, the re-drive pattern's portability constraint, or the gather.mjs retry mirror. If the baseline is 're-frozen + documented,' the plan does not say WHERE or in what artifact. Existing docs/ADRs referencing the old coverage-map schema shape will be stale.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit AC: 'ADR-0057 is updated (or a new ADR-0058 created) documenting: the new per-step `advanced`/`path` schema fields and their semantics; the re-drive portability contract (gate on trace flag, not prototype literal); the gather.mjs arrival-retry pattern and its parity with conformance. The frozen determinism baseline re-freeze is logged in the ADR with the commit ref.'

findings.location: AC1: 'Coverage-map trace steps carry a per-step `advanced` (and/or `path`) signal, re-recorded'; SCOPE: 'requires RE-RECORDING the coverage map … and re-freezing the determinism baseline'
findings.finding: The coverage map is shared/global persistent state with a well-known lifecycle concern exposed by this plan. (a) CREATE/UPDATE ownership: the plan states re-recording is 'LLM-driven exploration' but does NOT specify who triggers it, under what conditions, or who owns the write — a concurrent re-record (e.g., two CI runs simultaneously re-recording) could corrupt the map. (b) CONSUME: the plan does not enumerate all consumers that read the coverage map and verify they are tolerant of the new schema (absent `advanced` field) during the transition period. (c) RETIRE: there is no cleanup path if the re-recorded map is invalid or if old snapshots need to be purged. (d) Concurrency safety: re-recording is a non-atomic multi-step write (LLM exploration → freeze); if interrupted, the map may be left in a partially-written or inconsistent state with no stated recovery mechanism.
findings.suggested_edit: Add lifecycle ACsthat cover: (1) a single designated owner/trigger for re-recording (e.g., 'only the designated coverage-refresh CI job writes the map; concurrent writes are prevented by a lock file or branch protection'); (2) an enumeration of all consumers and confirmation they tolerate absent `advanced` fields during transition; (3) a rollback/recovery step if re-recording is interrupted ('restore from the last frozen snapshot at <ref>'); (4) a concurrency guard (e.g., atomic rename of the new map file, or a CI mutex).

findings.location: Plan overall — CONTEXT, SCOPE, Acceptance Criteria
All five AC bullets
AC1: 'anchoring on the legend OR on option-domain coverage'; AC2: 'robust value-synonyms / a polarity rule that ignores a shared head token'
findings.finding:
Two scope-boundary ambiguities block unambiguous execution:

1. AC1 presents two independent resolution strategies joined by 'OR' without specifying which takes priority, when to fall back from the first to the second, or whether both must be implemented. An agent must choose an architecture (legend-anchoring vs. option-domain-coverage) without guidance — this is a deferred design decision embedded in an AC.

2. AC2 says 'robust value-synonyms / a polarity rule that ignores a shared head token' but does not define what 'ignores a shared head token' means algorithmically (e.g., exclude a token from scoring if it appears in ≥N options, subtract its weight, skip tokens present in all candidates, etc.). The mechanism is left for the executor to invent.
findings.suggested_edit: For AC1: State explicitly whether both strategies are required (legend-anchor is attempted first; option-domain coverage is the fallback), or only one is needed. For AC2: Specify the rule precisely, e.g., 'a token shared by all candidate options is excluded from polarity scoring (count ≥ total options).'

findings.location: AC1, AC2, AC3, AC4, AC5 — the plan body contains no 'Steps' or implementation decomposition section
findings.finding:
The plan body describes the problem and its two root causes in detail but contains NO described implementation steps whatsoever. Consequently:

(ac_to_step) None of the five ACs map to a described implementation step — the plan jumps directly from problem diagnosis to acceptance criteria with nothing in between.

(proving_command) AC1 ('resolves its determinative control'), AC2 ('4 abstract immigration values bind … distinctly'), and AC3 ('A-4c documented RED is flipped to GREEN') each imply a concrete test command (e.g., running scenario-match-test.mjs and observing a GREEN result), but no such command is named in the plan.

(end_state_reachable) Without steps, there is no union-of-steps to evaluate for end-state reachability; the gap between 'what we'll do' and 'what done looks like' is total.

AC4 references 'existing 46 scenario-match checks' and a lint command (ENGINE_FILES), which are close to proving commands, but they are not attached to any step.
findings.suggested_edit: Add an explicit Steps or Implementation Plan section that, at minimum: (1) describes the legend-OR-domain-coverage anchoring change in groupCandidates/pickBest and names the file(s) to edit; (2) describes the shared-head-token polarity fix and the synonym table update; (3) names the command to run to verify AC3 (e.g., 'node scenario-match-test.mjs — expect 0 RED, previously 1 RED for mixed-status'); (4) names the regression command for AC4.

findings.location: Plan body (What section) vs. AC1–AC5
findings.finding: Bidirectional coverage failure: The plan body identifies two root causes — (1) legend too generic, (2) verbose option labels / polarity tie — but the body contains no described deliverables or implementation actions at all. The ACs name outcomes (e.g., 'option-domain coverage', 'polarity rule that ignores a shared head token') that reference implementation constructs never introduced or named in the body. This means the ACs have no covering work description, and the described problem analysis has no corresponding AC for verifying that the root-cause fix was actually applied (only the symptom-level outcome is verified). Additionally, AC5 ('Closes A-4 AC2 for all three scenarios') partially overlaps with AC3 ('mixed-status resolves') since mixed-status is one of the three scenarios — this near-duplication means one scenario's resolution is verified by two separate criteria without the overlap being acknowledged.
findings.suggested_edit: Add a Deliverables or Approach section that names each concrete change (e.g., 'Add option-domain-coverage fallback to groupCandidates'; 'Update polarity scorer to discount shared-head tokens'; 'Expand value-synonym table for the four immigration labels'). Map each deliverable to its AC. Merge or clarify the overlap between AC3 and AC5.

findings.location: What section vs. AC1–AC5
findings.finding: No genuine cross-section contradictions found. The problem context (legend too generic; polarity tie on verbose options) is consistent with AC1 (fix legend anchoring) and AC2 (fix polarity rule). The regression guard in AC4 is consistent with the reference to existing 46 checks. The references section points to ADR-0039 and scenario-match.mjs, which align with the test-verification language in AC3–AC4.

findings.location: Title vs. AC1–AC5 and What section
findings.finding: The title ('matcher robustness — legend-or-option-domain anchoring + verbose-option value-synonyms (mixed-status on real snap)') is faithfully reflected by the body: the two named mechanisms (legend/domain anchoring and verbose-option synonyms) correspond exactly to AC1 and AC2; the named target scenario (mixed-status on real snap) corresponds to AC3; and AC4 protects the callers of the existing matcher by requiring the 46 existing checks to stay green. No scope drift, no contradiction of intent detected.

findings.location: Plan body — 'Two compounding causes' + AC list (5 ACs)

AC3: 'mixed-status resolves on the real captured citizenship controls; the A-4c documented RED test is flipped to GREEN' and AC2: 'The 4 abstract immigration values bind to the 4 real verbose option labels distinctly'
findings.finding: The plan's test strategy for AC2 relies on verifying that the 4 abstract values bind 'distinctly' to verbose option labels, but does not specify any failure/ambiguity path test — e.g. what happens when two options share a head token ('citizen') after the new polarity rule is applied, or when a value-synonym produces a tie. The only articulated negative test is the single existing RED→GREEN flip (AC3). There is no coverage of: (a) what the matcher returns when option-domain coverage is partial or absent (no-candidate or wrong candidate), (b) what happens if a new group legend is equally generic for a different question type, or (c) regression for the 4 distinct bindings when option text changes. This risks a self-oracle pattern: the test is written to match the current real snap output rather than asserting the contract (each of the 4 abstract values maps to exactly one distinct option with no ambiguous tie). The absence of a collision/ambiguity-guard test for the verbose-label path is a gap given that AC2 explicitly calls out 'existing collision/ambiguity guards intact' — yet no test verifying those guards fire is mentioned.
findings.suggested_edit: Add explicit test cases for: (1) a tie-producing scenario where two options share the head token — assert the guard fires and returns no-match or an error rather than a random pick; (2) a partial domain-coverage scenario — assert the group is NOT selected as the determinative control; (3) the 4-binding test should assert each abstract value maps to EXACTLY one option and that no two abstract values map to the same option, not merely that 'resolution succeeds'.

findings.location: Plan overall — matcher logic operates on already-captured snapshots, no new I/O introduced
Plan overall — no external integrations, writes, or stateful transitions introduced; operates on in-memory snap data
Plan overall — purely internal matcher/resolver logic; no endpoints, PII handling, or auth boundaries introduced
AC1: 'anchoring on the legend OR on option-domain coverage' and AC2: 'robust value-synonyms / a polarity rule that ignores a shared head token'
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new matching strategies (legend-OR-option-domain anchoring, and a modified polarity rule that ignores shared head tokens). These are new business rules embedded in the matcher pipeline. It is unclear whether these thresholds/rules (e.g. what constitutes 'collective domain coverage', the definition of 'shared head token') will be configurable or hardcoded. The plan references ADR-0039 for context but does not state whether a new ADR entry or design-doc update will capture the new anchoring strategy and the modified polarity rule. Without visibility into the codebase it cannot be confirmed whether these rules are isolated behind a named interface or silently woven into existing logic. If hardcoded, they will be fragile when real snap option text changes again.
findings.suggested_edit: Explicitly state in the plan whether (a) the 'option-domain coverage' threshold (e.g. minimum fraction of question-type values covered) is a named constant or parameter, and (b) the modified polarity rule's 'shared head token' detection is encapsulated in a named function. Add a note that ADR-0039 will be updated (or a new ADR created) to document the two new anchoring strategies.

findings.location: AC3: 'the A-4c documented RED test is flipped to GREEN'
AC4: 'No regression: the existing 46 scenario-match checks + the alien-mock portability stay green'
